coredata.dc:title: An overview of recent progress in nanostructured carbon-based supercapacitor electrodes: From zero to bi-dimensional materials
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Rajesh
Joanni, Ednan
Sahoo, Sumanta
Shim, Jae-Jin
Tan, Wai Kian
Matsuda, Atsunori
Singh, Rajesh K.
coredata.dc:description: The lack of higher performance energy storage has been widely acknowledged as a major factor hindering further developments in transportation, portable and wearable electronic devices, among other key applications. Although supercapacitors (SCs) have been known for more than fifty years, only recently these devices have been considered as promising candidates to fulfil this significant technology gap. Developments in nanotechnology and manufacturing techniques applied to high-performance advanced electrode materials have accelerated progress in this fast-moving field. In this comprehensive overview article, we systematically survey the current state of art on fabrication and the corresponding electrochemical performance of electrode materials for SCs. The text covers novel carbon nanomaterials having different dimensions, such as 0D-carbon quantum dots, 1D-carbon nanostructures (carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, carbon nano yarns and their composites) as well as 2D-carbon materials (graphene, doped-graphene, graphene derivatives and their composites). Emerging fabrication technologies are also addressed, including conventional SCs, as well as asymmetric, flexible, micro, stretchable and wearable devices. Additionally, the drawbacks for each class of electrode material, the major challenges facing the current technologies, and some of the promising research directions in this field have also been discussed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The lack of higher performance energy storage has been widely acknowledged as a major factor hindering further developments in transportation, portable and wearable electronic devices, among other key applications. Although supercapacitors (SCs) have been known for more than fifty years, only recently these devices have been considered as promising candidates to fulfil this significant technology gap. Developments in nanotechnology and manufacturing techniques applied to high-performance advanced electrode materials have accelerated progress in this fast-moving field. In this comprehensive overview article, we systematically survey the current state of art on fabrication and the corresponding electrochemical performance of electrode materials for SCs. The text covers novel carbon nanomaterials having different dimensions, such as 0D-carbon quantum dots, 1D-carbon nanostructures (carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, carbon nano yarns and their composites) as well as 2D-carbon materials (graphene, doped-graphene, graphene derivatives and their composites). Emerging fabrication technologies are also addressed, including conventional SCs, as well as asymmetric, flexible, micro, stretchable and wearable devices. Additionally, the drawbacks for each class of electrode material, the major challenges facing the current technologies, and some of the promising research directions in this field have also been discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: The gradual, but ever-growing human aspiration for a pollution-free environment generates a heavy demand for development and implementation of “clean” and sustainable energy cycles. Improvements in electrical energy handling are also essential for mankind to accelerate technological developments in several other key areas, such as transportation, communications and personal electronic devices, among others [1–10]. Energy-related discussions are mainly confined to issues that can ultimately be summarized by three simple expressions: “safe production”, “fast conversion” and “high storage” [11–17]. The later one is currently more important, because of the pressing need to store higher amounts of charge in smaller volumes [18–22]. Energy storage devices, such as supercapacitors (SCs) and rechargeable batteries have attracted the attention of researchers and engineers striving to promote a clean environment [23–29]. The large scale manufacture of highly efficient energy storage devices for use in transportation can help to replace non-renewable energy sources. Also, the future depletion of fossils fuels is expected to induce the adoption of electrical energy collected from solar, wind, hydro and geothermal sources. This energy must be stored in various kinds of SCs and batteries for subsequent use.

SCs are well known for their distinctive energy storage characteristics, combining high capacity, high power density, fast charge/discharge processes and long cycling life. The energy storage capacity of SCs is very high (when compared to conventional dielectric capacitors), but is still much smaller than that of a typical secondary battery [30]. Presently, application of SCs as power sources in electric vehicles, portable and wearable electronic devices are the most visible ones. The potential for further application of SCs is actually much bigger, ranging from microscopic-sized elements powering wearable devices, up to industrial scale units smoothing fluctuations from demand and supply in nationwide power grids.

The performance of devices is highly dependent on the nature of the electrode materials, thus selection of materials for SC electrodes is a big challenge [31–35]. In advanced SC devices, novel carbon nanomaterials modified with different kinds of metal oxides or conducting polymers have been used as electrodes [36]. Recently, researchers have also introduced MXenes and chalcogenide-based materials in electrodes for SCs. Both the composition and the assembled structure of materials in the electrodes determine the capacitance, energy/power density, rate capability and long term stability of SC devices [37]. Continuous research efforts have been applied to develop electrode materials containing nano-sized pores. The goal is to increase the specific surface area, allowing easy access of electrolyte and shortening ionic diffusion lengths, in order to improve the performance of SCs [38–42].

In this comprehensive overview article, we have focused the discussion on recent studies of various new carbon-based electrode materials designed for high performance SCs. We have covered electrodes for SCs comprising a wide range of nanostructured carbon: carbon dots (CDs); carbon quantum dots (CQDs); graphene quantum dots (GQDs); single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs); multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs); carbon fibers (CFs); carbon nanofibers (CNFs); graphene; graphene nanoribbons (GNRs); graphene oxide (GO); reduced graphene oxide (rGO); functionalized graphene; graphene aerogel and graphene hydrogel, as well as the corresponding doped materials [43–48]. Hybrid electrodes combining two or more materials into 1D or 2D morphologies have demonstrated improved charge storage characteristics. The SCs made from hybrid electrode materials exhibit superior performances in terms of energy/power density and long term stability [49–51]. Different research groups have studied various kinds of metal oxides (Co3O4, NiO, Fe2O3/Fe3O2, MnO2 etc.) combined with graphene-based materials as promising high performance electrodes for SCs [52–56]. A schematic drawing showing various types of carbon materials used in electrodes for SCs is depicted in Fig. 1 .

Taking into account the energy storage mechanisms, SCs can be classified as electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) or pseudocapacitors. In EDLCs, the capacitance derives from electrostatic charges accumulated at the electrode/electrolyte interface, the so-called “electric double-layer”. On the other hand, in a pseudocapacitor, the energy storage takes place by Faradaic redox reactions, involving electronic charge transfer between the electrodes and the electrolyte. In most cases, the maximum charge in both types of devices is related to the electrode surface area which is accessible to the electrolyte ions [62–67], but this is not the only factor dictating the electrochemical performance of SCs. The surface morphology and electrical conductivity are also important parameters which may determine the feasibility of pseudocapacitive materials for designing SC electrodes [68–76]. For the electrodes in EDLCs, carbon materials with different dimensions and morphologies have been extensively studied [77–84]. For pseudocapacitors, mainly the metal oxides and hydroxides, conducting polymers, chalcogenides and MXenes have been recently investigated. Mass loading of electrode materials plays a vital role in SC applications, with high mass loading of pseudocapacitive materials being necessary for achieving good electrochemical performance.

In general terms, pseudocapacitive materials can be placed between EDLCs and batteries. In some cases, it is even difficult to distinguish between pseudocapacitive and battery materials, since both exhibit fast redox kinetics. This has generated some debates in the scientific literature, but the controversy has been alleviated with the help from theoretical review articles and editorial notes recently published on this matter [85–89].

Even though pseudocapacitors usually exhibit higher capacitance than EDLCs, they tend to have lower cycling stability due to the mechanical degradation caused by swelling/shrinkage during the charge/discharge processes. Carbon-based materials and pseudo-capacitive materials have been combined to develop hybrid SCs, which incorporate the charge-storage characteristics of both. In fact, hybrid SCs are able to achieve higher energy densities than EDLCs and pseudocapacitors [90–92], usually keeping a good cycling performance. The different types of SC electrode materials, arranged according to their charge storage mechanisms, are schematically represented in Fig. 2 .

Basically, the pseudocapacitance exploited in SCs involves one of three possible mechanisms: (i) intercalation, (ii) controlled redox reaction, and (iii) doping/un-doping of conducting polymers. However, from another point of view, pseudocapacitors can also be classified into two classes - (i) intrinsic and (ii) extrinsic. The extrinsic materials only behave as pseudocapacitors when their dimensions are reduced down to the nanoscale, while the intrinsic materials are not subjected to this limitation. A detailed account of the classification, properties, and importance of the types of pseudocapacitor materials has been presented in a recent review article [93]. Fig. 3 shows a schematic illustration of the two kinds of pseudocapacitors, as well as graphs depicting the typical electrochemical behavior of pseudocapacitive materials.

Following the current research trends, SC devices with different materials and configurations have been extensively studied. The devices can be classified into three main categories – symmetric (both positive and negative electrodes are from same material), asymmetric (combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitive electrodes), and hybrid (combination of capacitive and battery-type electrodes) [68,86,92,94]. In general, asymmetric devices exhibit higher capacitance than symmetric ones. Based on the electrolytes, the SCs can be classified as – aqueous, quasi-solid state, and solid-state devices. Solid-state devices are generally constructed by using gel electrolytes. Beside these, organic electrolytes and ionic liquid-based devices has also been constructed. When compared to the aqueous, solid-state devices possess higher working potential. In recent times, a few different types of devices have also been investigated. For example, all-pseudocapacitive asymmetric devices have been fabricated by using pseudocapacitive materials as both positive and negative electrodes [95]. On the other hand, flexible and wearable SC devices have also been developed [96]. Using EDLC and pseudocapacitive electrodes, micro-SC devices were also fabricated [97–103]. Further, using conducting polymer derivatives, all-transparent stretchable electrochromic SC devices have been constructed [104]. Moreover, other types of devices such as print-based, paper-based, fiber-shaped, ionogel-based were also developed [105–112]. The electrochemical performances of these and other types of devices are discussed in this review article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 0D carbon-based materials for supercapacitors: quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Among the different kinds of QDs, carbon-based QDs (CDs, CQDs, GQDs and their heteroatom-doped derivatives) have found application in SCs due to their water solubility, chemical stability, simple functionalization chemistry, low toxicity and excellent electro-optical characteristics [113–118].

The CDs are a new family of carbon particles with nanosized structure (<10 nm), usually consisting of a nanocrystalline core and an amorphous shell containing abundant surface functional groups [119]. Athika et al. [120] synthesized CQDs derived from denatured milk by hydrothermal method for SCs applications. The CQD electrodes displayed a considerable specific capacitance of 95 F/g with excellent stability as well as coulombic efficiency over 1000 cycles. Lately, several reports have recognized that CDs could enhance SC performance by addition of carbon nanomaterials containing a different dimensionality. Dang et al. [121] reported fabrication of CDs anchored to rGO via covalent bonding for SCs. The addition of an appropriate quantity of CDs to rGO (CQDs:rGO = 5:1) brought significant enhancement to the electrochemical performance of SCs, with good rate capability and high specific capacitance (211.9 F/g), which was 74.3% higher than that of rGO electrodes. Zhao et al. [122] demonstrated synthesis of CD-decorated rGO to form an interconnected 3D network using a hydrothermal method. The electrode exhibited specific capacitance of 308 F/g (0.5 A/g) and 92% capacitance retention over 20000 cycles (at 10 A/g). Feng et al. [123] prepared ultra-small CDs attached to rGO hydrogels by hydrothermal method. CDs promote electron transport, reducing the internal resistance of the 3D interconnected rGO hydrogel/CDs network. The flexible solid-state SC (130 μm thick rGO hydrogel/CD electrode) delivered specific capacitance of 264 F/g (up to 301 F/g for 40 μm thick electrode), areal specific capacitance of 394 mF/cm2 (432 F/cm2 for 200 μm thick electrode), good cycling stability (9.1% deterioration after 5000 cycles), large energy density (35.3 Wh/kg) and high power density (516 W/kg). Hoang et al. [124] investigated zucchini-derived CDs which were used to prepare 3D porous rGO/CD composites for electrodes in SC applications. The rGO/CD composites exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 374 F/g obtained at 2 mV/s with a capacitance retention of 71.7% as the scan rate increased from 2 to 1000 mV/s. Also, rGO/CDs composites exhibited 93.8% capacitance retention over 10000 cycles (at 10 A/g).

Recent reports reveal that CDs have also been anchored/decorated on nanoparticles and polymers to enhance the electrochemical performance of SCs [125–130]. De et al. [125] reported synthesis of GO hydrogel decorated by CD-stabilized CuS nanoparticles using hydrothermal method (180 °C) for asymmetric SCs. The CDs help to strongly bind the CuS nanoparticles to GO inside the 3D hydrogel structure. The electrode exhibited specific capacitance of 920 F/g (at 1 A/g). The synthesized material was used as a positive electrode for the fabrication of asymmetric SCs along with rGO as the negative electrode. The SCs delivered energy densities up to 28 W h/kg, retaining up to 90% specific capacitance after 5000 cycles. Wei et al. [126] investigated CQDs-decorated Ni(OH)2 with lamellar structure by hydrothermal method (at 200 °C for 1.5 h) using Ni(Ac)2·4H2O and glycerol. The specific capacitance of CQDs/Ni(OH)2 reaches 2900 F/g at 1 A/g and the capacitance remains 2051 F/g at the high current density of 10 A/g. Lv et al. [127] explained that CQDs serve as the bridge for connecting MnO2 and graphene, contributing to synthesize a stable MnO2/CQDs/graphene aerogel (GA). The composite GA has a 3D network structure, a large specific surface area and good electron transport pathways. The MnO2/CQDs/GA electrode exhibits excellent electrochemical performance (when compared to MnO2/GA and MnO2/G) and displays a specific capacitance of 721 F/g (1 A/g), good rate capability (89.2% capacitance at 20 A/g) and 92.3% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles at 10 A/g. Zhang et al. [128] synthesized ternary composites containing CDs, polypyrrole (PPy) and GO via in-situ polymerization under mild conditions for SC applications. The authors have suggested that CDs with large specific surface area can enhance the interfacial properties among GO, CDs and PPy, increasing the dielectric constant of the ternary composite. The synthesized CD/PPy/GO exhibited high specific capacitance of 576 F/g (at 0.5 A/g), and a SC with symmetric GO/CDs/PPy electrodes shows energy density of 30.1 Wh/kg at a power density of 250 W/kg with good stability after 5000 cycles. Jian et al. [129] studied the synthesis of CQDs-reinforced PPy nanowires via electrostatic self-assembly. Fig. 4 a shows a schematic diagram for preparation of CQDs/PPy nanowire composites in which the good conductivity of CQDs reduced the charge transfer resistance and increased the rate of electrochemical reaction in the PPy nanowires. The synthesized CQDs/PPy nanowire composites demonstrated high electrochemical performance, with specific capacitance of 306 F/g (0.5 A/g), retaining 66.8% of the capacitance when the current density was increased to 40 A/g. Also, the fabricated symmetric SCs exhibited an areal capacitance of 248.5 mF/cm2 at 0.2 mA/cm2 and good cycling stability at the current density of 5 mA/cm2 (85.2% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles) (Fig. 4b and c). Jian et al. [130] demonstrated the fabrication of flexible all-solid-state SCs by co-deposition of CQDs with pyrrole monomer in aqueous solution. Fig. 4d, shows the schematic representation of CQDs/PPy composite formation on the surface of stainless steel wire mesh via electrochemical polymerization. The electrodeposition time plays a crucial role in controlling the morphology of the CQDs/PPy composite film. The as-prepared CQDs/PPy composite exhibited an areal capacitance of 315 mF/cm2 (specific capacitance of 308 F/g) at a current density of 0.2 mA/cm2 and 85.7% capacitance retention after 2 000 cycles (Fig. 4 e,f).

N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with abundant amino groups have also been used with polyaniline (PANI) to enhance the performance of SCs. Wang et al. [131] obtained flake-shaped N-CQDs/PANI nanocomposites via rapid-mixing polymerization for application as electrode materials in SCs. The N-CQDs were synthesized via a one-step process in a domestic microwave oven (700 W, 4 min) with citric acid as the carbon source and 1,2-ethylenediamine as the surface passivation agent. The electrochemical analysis revealed that all of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites exhibited higher specific capacitances than PANI alone. Especially, N-CQD/PANI prepared at an aniline concentration of 0.02 mol/L with a feeding ratio of N-CQDs to aniline of 10 wt%, possessed the highest electrical conductivity (11.11 S/cm), specific capacitance (498.3 F/g at 1 A/g), rate capability and cycling stability when compared to other proportions of N-CQDs and aniline.

The GQDs and its derivatives are regarded as good electrode materials for energy storage in SCs due to their high electrical conductivity, high surface area and excellent dispersion in various solvents [129,132,133]. There has been a growing interest in new GQD composites for SCs in recent years. Chen et al. [134] described GQDs deposition onto 3D graphene by a benign electrochemical method and the composite materials were used as electrodes for symmetrical SCs. The SCs fabricated from GQD/3D graphene composite were highly stable and exhibited specific capacitance of 268 F/g with more than 90% improvement over that of the SCs made from pure 3D graphene electrodes (136 F/g). Niu et al. [135] suggested that the specific capacitance and rate performance is enhanced when GQDs are mixed with carbon black. The authors have found that electrode materials incorporating this mixture yielded specific capacitance values of 100 F/g at a current density of 0.1 A/g, and 85 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g. Islam et al. [136] performed in-situ direct grafting of GQDs onto CFs via covalent ester linkages at low temperature (90 °C). The flexible fabric SCs showed increased electrochemical capacitance (about 5.5 times) when compared to CF. The SCs maintained ∼97% of its initial capacitance after 5000 cycles (at 2 A/g). Huang et al. [137] studied the synthesis of GQDs-decorated hollow NiCo2S4 nanowires via hydrothermal approach as shown in Fig. 5 a. The GQDs/NiCo2S4 nanocomposite shows high specific capacitance (678.2 F/g at 0.2 A/g) and good cycling stability (94% retention after 5000 cycles) as shown in Fig. 5b and c, respectively. The authors have suggested that the improved performance should be due to lower impedance, larger specific surface area, more interspace and pathways for both ion diffusion and volume changes. Mondal et al. [138] prepared GQD-doped PANI (GQD–PANI) composites by chemical oxidation of aniline as shown in Fig. 5d. Fig. 5e and f shows that the synthesized composites presented specific capacitance of 1044 F/g (at 1 A/g) with retention of 80.1% of this value after 3000 cycles. Luo et al. [139] prepared GQDs encapsulated NiCo2O4 composite for SC electrodes. The as-prepared NiCo2O4@GQDs composite exhibited specific capacitance of 1242 F/g (at 30 A/g) which is higher than that of pristine NiCo2O4 (790 F/g at 30 A/g) and displays 99% capacitance retention after 4000 cycles. The assembled asymmetric SCs with AC as negative electrode possess specific energy density of 38 W h/kg at a power density of 800 W/kg.

Heteroatom-doped GQDs/GO-QDs have being highly used to improve the capacitive performance of SC electrodes to due to their distinct structure and properties that can be costumized by changing doping levels [140,141]. Li et al. [142] reported amine-enriched GQDs for SCs. The amine-enriched porous carbon electrodes were synthesized by electrostatic fusion of amine-functionalized single-crystalline GQDs. The carbon films presented high specific capacitance (400–595 F/g) and symmetrical GQD SCs can deliver energy densities up to 21.8 Wh/kg and retain 90% of the initial capacitance after 10000 CV tests. Xu et al. [143] described N-enriched hybrid graphene nanosheets derived from GO-QDs by photochemical doping for high-performance SCs. The GO nanosheets and GO-QDs were used as a structure-directing agent and in-situ activating agent, respectively, after photoreduction under NH3 atmosphere. Compared with rGO and N-rGO, the N-rGO/GO-QD electrodes exhibited enhanced electrochemical characteristics, with specific capacitance of 344 F/g (0.25 A/g) and 82% capacitance retention after 3000 cycles (at 0.8 A/g). The N-rGO/GO-QD electrodes also delivered high energy density (43 Wh/kg) at a power density of 417 W/kg in 1 M Li2SO4 electrolyte. Ganganboina et al. [144] suggested preparation of N-GQD-anchored Fe3O4-halloysite nanotubes (N-GQD@Fe3O4-HNTs), as active anode materials for SC applications. The authors have suggested that Fe3O4-HNTs provide a 1D matrix that shortens the diffusion path of electrons and electrolyte ions. This framework also helps to absorb the mechanical stress during cycling along with excess sites for charge storage. Besides enhancing the electrical conductivity of Fe3O4-HNTs, N-GQDs offer abundant and easily accessible electroactive sites for rapid electron and electrolyte ion transport. The N-GQD@Fe3O4-HNTs displays specific capacitance of 418 F/g and keeps good rate capability with energy and power densities of 10.4–29 W h/kg and 0.25–5.2 kW/kg, respectively.

Co-doped GQDs have been also tested as electrode materials in energy-related devices. Li et al. [145] investigated assembling of N and O co-doped GQDs onto carbon networks for all-solid-state flexible SCs (Fig. 6 a). The 3D CNT/carbon cloth (CC) network was used as a conductive, flexible and free-standing scaffold for the electro-deposition of highly N and O co-doped GQDs (N–O-GQDs). The electrodes exhibited an areal capacitance of 461 mF/cm2 (at 0.5 mA/cm2), areal energy density of 32 μWh/cm2 and capacitance retention of 87.5% after 2000 charge−discharge cycles as shown in Fig. 6b–d, respectively. In another publication, Li et al. [146] described the electrophoretic construction of carbon-based micro SCs using heavily co-doped N–O-GQDs (17.8 at% N and 21.3 at% O), which were synthesized by a modified molecular fusion method. The N and O co-doped GQDs offer a combination of advantages, such as plentiful active sites, high hydrophilicity and easy assembly into conductive carbon films. The N–O-GQD micro SCs showed volumetric capacitance of 325 F/cm3 due to the large amount of doped nitrogen and oxygen functional groups.

N–S co-doped GQD-decorated metal oxide/PANI for hybrid SC electrodes synthesized by Oskueyan et al. [147]. The GQDs were grown on CeO2 nanoparticles via an in-situ hydrothermal method. The PANI-N-S-GQDs@CeO2 nanocomposites were prepared by a chemical oxidation polymerization process. The maximum specific capacity of 189 C/g (at 1 A/g) was obtained for PANI/5 wt% N–S-GQDs@CeO2 nanocomposites in comparison with 175 C/g and 115 C/g for PANI/10 wt% N–S-GQDs@CeO2 and PANI/5 wt% CeO2, respectively. The specific capacity was lowered to 75% of the original value after 1000 cycles (at 1 A/g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 1D carbon-based materials for supercapacitors: carbon nanotubes/nanofibers
ce:section.ce:para: The 1D carbon-based materials (CNTs, CFs/CNFs and carbon yarns/carbon nanoyarns etc.) have received great attention due to their physical and chemical properties which render these materials as promising candidates for electrodes in SCs [148–162]. Composites incorporating these 1D materials have also been applied to the field of SCs, showing improved electrochemical properties [163–174].

The application of CNT-based materials for high-performance electrodes in SCs is one of the promising approaches for achieving high energy density and high power density. However, large scale production and post-treatment of CNT-based materials with high purity are essential for commercial adoption of high-performance SCs based on CNT electrodes [175–178].

CNTs possess good electrical conductivity and excellent mechanical and electrochemical properties. In several studies published in the scientific literature, pristine CNTs have been utilized as electrode materials for SCs, providing EDLC behavior [176,179–181]. He et al. [182] measured the electrochemical performance of biocompatible CNTs for SCs, obtaining volumetric capacitance of 10.4 F/cm3 with 98.3% retention after 10000 cycles in phosphate buffer saline. Lei et al. [183] demonstrated in-situ growth (on 316L stainless steel) of self-supported and defective CNT networks via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and thermal treatment to generate binder-free SCs. The CNT networks annealed at 500 °C for 2 h displayed a capacitance of 11 mF/cm2 and 97% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles at 0.5 mA/cm2.

CNTs grown in the inner walls of carbonized wood tracheids using Ni catalyst have been obtained by Wu et al. for SCs applications [184]. The authors have found that capacitance values were approximately five times higher than those of activated wood carbon, being equal to or higher than that of SCs based on wood carbon slices with pseudocapacitive materials. The specific surface area increased from 365.5 to 537.9 m2/g and the specific capacitance was improved to 215.3 F/g. The energy density of the all-solid-state SCs was 39.8 Wh/kg, and 96.2% of the capacitance was retained after 10000 charging/discharging cycles. Qiu et al. [185] suggested that functionalizing CNTs with ferrocenyl improved the surface reactivity, enhancing the capacitance of electrode materials. The ferrocenyl-functionalized CNTs exhibited a capacitance of 53.6 F/g at a current density of 0.4 A/g. The symmetric ferrocenyl-functionalized CNT SCs show higher energy density than CNT SCs. Capacitance retention values of 96.0% after 5000 cycles have also been measured. Zhou et al. [186] synthesized open-ended CNTs with carbon microtubes by a CVD method for SC electrodes with EDLC behavior. The SCs displayed 98% capacitance retention after 10000 charge/discharge cycles. The maximum energy density was 70.9 Wh/kg at a power density of 583 W/kg and remained 28.6 Wh/kg at a higher power density of 8.57 kW/kg. In another study, Zhou et al. [187] reported the synthesis of branched CNTs/CNFs by CVD method for SC electrodes. The electrochemical analysis indicated that SCs based on branched CNTs/CNFs display specific capacitances up to 207 F/g (at 1 A/g), performing better than branched CNTs. The CNTs/CNFs-based electrode materials show good cycling stability of 95.6% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles (at 20 A/g) and energy density of 63.7 Wh/kg in EMIBF4 organic electrolyte.

Hierarchical CNTs/N-doped porous carbon for high-performance SCs studied by Zhou et al. [188]. The CNTs promote rapid ion transfer and storage during charge/discharge processes. The CNTs/N-doped porous carbon composite demonstrated considerable specific capacitance of 293.1 F/g (1 A/g) and high rate capability (207 F/g at 30 A/g). The assembled symmetric SCs delivered an energy density of 27.46 Wh/kg at the power density of 874.98 W/kg. Yang et al. [189] performed in-situ growth of CNTs derived from MOFs using 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid, N,N dimethylmethanamide and C4H6NiO4 at 800 °C in N2 atmosphere. The authors used the resulting material for solid-state SC electrodes. The areal capacitances were 272 mF/cm2 and 168 mF/cm2 at 0.5 mA/cm2 and 10 mA/cm2, respectively. Capacitance retention was 100%, even after 5000 charge/discharge cycles, as shown in Fig. 7 .

In the last few years, transition metal oxides combined with CNTs have been synthesized for application in energy storage/conversion due to their promising electronic and electrochemical performances [190–194]. Among the transition metal oxides, Co3O4 nanoparticles/nanostructures integrated with 1D carbon-based materials is a favorable candidate for SCs electrodes. The composite nanomaterial has high theoretical specific capacitance, good redox performance and easily controllable size and shape [195–198]. Shan et al. [198] synthesized MWCNT/Co3O4 nanocomposites through acid treatment of MWNTs and in-situ decomposition of Co(NO3)2 in n-hexanol solution at 140 °C. The MWCNTs/Co3O4 composite displayed specific capacitance of 200.98 F/g, which is higher than that of pure MWCNTs (90.1 F/g). Ke et al. [199] developed the synthesis of nanohybrids for SC electrodes consisting of Co3O4 nanowire arrays on Ni foam decorated with functionalized CNTs. Nanohybrid assembly was driven by the strong electrostatic interaction between carboxyl groups and amine groups in functionalized CNTs and amine-modified Co3O4 nanowire arrays (Fig. 8 a). The capacitance of functionalized CNTs-decorated Co3O4 reached 559 F/g, which was about 3.5 times higher than that of pristine Co3O4 nanowires (Fig. 8b). The original capacitance was retained upon 1000 charge/discharge cycles (Fig. 8c).

Co3O4 nanoflakes on SWCNT thin films were prepared by Durukan et al. [200] and tested as SC electrodes. The films were prepared by vacuum filtration and consecutive stamping method onto which Co(OH)2 nanoflakes were electrodeposited and annealed to obtain Co3O4 nanoflakes. The specific capacitance of the SWCNT-Co3O4 nanoflake electrode materials was 313.9 F/g, corresponding to an areal specific capacitance of 70.5 mF/cm2 at a scan rate of 1 mV/s. Capacity retention was 80% after 3000 cycles. Sun et al. [201] designed Co3O4 nanowire arrays with hierarchical structure grown on CNT fiber hybrids as cathode materials for wearable fiber-shaped asymmetric SCs. The SCs were lightweight and flexible energy storage devices with potential applications in portable and wearable electronics. The hybrid fibers showed volumetric capacitance of 734.25 F/cm3 (areal capacitance: 2.21 F/cm2) in a three-electrode system. The fabricated all-solid-state fiber-shaped asymmetric SCs (positive electrode: Co3O4 nanowire/CNTs fibers; negative electrode: vanadium nitride nanowires/CNTs fibers) possessed energy density of 13.2 mWh/cm3 at a current density of 1.0 A cm3 in KOH/PVA gel electrolyte. Kazazi et al. [202] reported pseudocapacitive behavior of electrodeposited porous Co3O4 films on electrophoretically modified graphite electrodes with CNTs. CNTs were deposited on the surface of graphite sheet (cathodic electrophoretic deposition technique) from their ethanolic suspension. Thin films of Co3O4 were electrodeposited on CNT coated graphite substrate by a galvanostatic method, followed by a thermal treatment in air. The composite electrode provided areal capacitance of 4.96F/cm2 at a current density of 2 mA/cm2, rate capability of 64.7% (capacitance retention from 2 mA/cm2 to 50 mA/cm2), and cycling stability with capacitance retention of 91.8% after 2000 cycles.

Among various transition-metal oxides for electrode materials in SCs, NiO combined with carbon-related materials has attracted attention because of its simple synthesis method, low cost, reversible redox reactions and high theoretical specific capacitance (2573 F/g) [203–211]. Wu et al. [212] developed porous CNT films with a supported NiO nanonet synthesized by electrophoretic deposition and hydrothermal method for SC applications. The material delivered specific capacitance of 1511 F/g at high discharge current (50 A/g) due to significantly improved transport of the electrolyte and electrons. Hakamada et al. [213] suggested MWCNT/NiO nanocomposites for SC electrodes. A modified Watts bath coupled with pulsed current electroplating was used to deposit nanosized NiO particles (diameter <4 nm) on the surfaces of MWCNTs. The capacitance of the MWCNTs/NiO electrodes was 2480 F/g (per mass of NiO), which was nearly close to the theoretical capacitance of NiO (2573 F/g). Wen et al. [214] designed core–shell structured nanocomposite of NiO with CNTs as positive electrode for SCs. The CNTs coated with NiO were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The materials showed specific capacitance of 1844 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g and 1145 F/g at 10 A/g, with 88.9% capacitance retention after 1000 cycles at 1 A/g. The specific capacitance of NiO was 972 F/g (at 1 A/g), which was almost half that of NiO/CNTs nanocomposite. For an asymmetric SC combining the composite with AC as the negative electrode, the energy density was up to 52.6 Wh/kg.

The use of carbon nanotubes as sacrificial templates for the fabrication of NiO nanotubes for SC electrodes has been demonstrated by Abdalla et al. [215]. Electroless deposition was applied to cover MWCNTs with a Ni layer. Subsequent annealing in the presence of air oxidized Ni to NiO and carbon was released as gaseous CO2, leaving behind NiO nanotubes. The materials showed specific capacitance of 245.3 F/g and areal capacitance of 3.28 F/cm2 at a scan rate of 2 mV/s. Majeed et al. [216] described carbon-encapsulated NiO nanoparticle decorated SWCNT thin films for binder-free flexible SC electrodes synthesized by the floating catalyst CVD technique and air oxidation. The binder-free flexible electrode material exhibited stable specific capacitance after 500 bending cycles at an angle of 130°. Specific capacitance was 1422 F/g with capacitance retention of 92% after 5000 cycles. Banerjee et al. [217] synthesized amorphous CNTs-NiO nanoflower hybrids for SC electrodes. Amorphous CNTs were grown by a low-temperature process and grafted with chemically synthesized NiO nanoflowers. The hybrids showed specific capacitance of 120 F/g, which was 12 times higher than that of pure amorphous CNTs. Nunes et al. [218] prepared NiO nanoparticles (diameter: ∼2–7 nm) supported onto radially oriented MWCNTs as electrodes for electrochemical SCs. Specific capacitances were 1200 F/g and 1028 F/g at current density of 5 A/g and 20 mV/s, respectively. Specific power was 140 Wh/kg and energy density was 9 W/kg. After 40000 charge/discharge cycles, the symmetric coin cell remained stable with high coulombic efficiency of 99%. Fig. 9 a–c and Fig. 9d–g shows SEM images of CNTs-NiO materials and electrochemical performance of CNTs-NiO electrodes, respectively. Morphological investigation revealed that 19% of the electrochemically active surface area was confined to the inner surface regions of the porous nanostructured active layer.

In SCs electrodes, Fe3O4 has been widely associated with CNTs due to advantages such as multiple oxidation states, very low toxicity, natural abundance and variable operating potential window [219–222]. Guan et al. [223] synthesized CNT/Fe3O4 nanocomposites by a hydrothermal processing for SCs electrode materials. Fe3O4 nano-cubes were well dispersed on the surfaces of the carbon nanotubes. The electrochemical analysis revealed that by using CNTs as supporting material for Fe3O4, the performance of SCs was improved. The CNTs/Fe3O4 nanocomposite exhibited specific capacitance of 117.2 F/g (10 mA/cm2), which is 3 times higher than Fe3O4 alone, and an energy density of 16.2 Wh/kg, with good stability during long term cycling. Li et al. [224] studied the performance of MWCNTs coated with Fe2O3 nanoparticles (Fe2O3@MWNTs) as electrode materials for SCs. Fe2O3 nanoparticles were deposited on the surface of MWNTs by atomic layer deposition. By applying a calcination process, the electrochemical performance of the Fe2O3@MWNTs nanocomposites was further enhanced. The Fe2O3@MWNTs nanocomposites exhibited specific capacitance of 787 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g and capacitance retention after 5000 cycles was 91.6%.

Modified Fe-doped PANI/sulfonated-MWCNTs (Fe-PANI@S-MWCNTs) obtained by a freeze-drying treatment for all-solid-state symmetric SCs (Fig. 10 a) has been investigated by El-Khodary et al. [225]. The Fe-PANI@S-MWCNTs electrode contained high specific capacitance of 2105.64 F/g at 1 A/g in 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. A symmetric solid-state SC device based on Fe-PANI@S-MWCNTs electrodes showed specific capacitance of 776.85 F/g at 1 A/g, keeping a good rate capability (59% retention at 20 A/g) (Fig. 10b). Cycling stability was characterized by a 91.6% capacitance retention after 500 cycles at 200 mV/s (Fig. 10c). Energy density was 13.55 Wh/kg, while the power density was 177.50 W/kg (Fig. 10 d). Atchudan et al. [226] described direct growth of Fe3O4 nanoparticle-MWCNTs (Fe3O4-MWCNTs) hybrids at 800 °C under atmospheric pressure for SC electrode materials. MWCNTs (using C2H2 gas) were synthesized by CVD and Fe3O4 by hydrothermal method. The SC electrode behavior of Fe3O4-MWCNTs hybrids was very stable and even after 5000 cycles of charge/discharge (at 4 A/g).

Among the transition metal oxides, MnO2 is considered a very promising and exciting material due to its high theoretical specific capacitance (1380 F/g), low cost, natural abundance and environmentally friendly nature [227–232]. Accordingly, nanocomposites combining carbon nanotubes and MnO2 have been investigated for SCs electrodes [233,234]. Su et al. [235] studied composites based on CNTs combined with MnO2 and with vanadium nitride for SC electrodes. The fabricated hybrid SC devices based on CNT-vanadium nitride negative electrodes and CNT-MnO2 positive electrodes revealed specific capacitance of 160 F/g at the scan rate of 2 mV/s. Wang et al. [236] also studied the formation of CNT/MnO2 composites for SCs. The CNTs were grafted with MnO2 using a three step process: (i) surface functionalization of the CNTs with carboxyl groups, (ii) grafting of Mn2+ with the carboxyl groups, (iii) and transformation of Mn2+ into MnO2 nanoparticles by KMnO4 oxidation. The bonds between the components are believed to form between the carbon from the carboxyl groups of the CNTs and the oxygen atoms of the MnO2 lattice. The CNTs/MnO2 composite revealed specific capacitance of 348 F/g at the scan rate of 5 mV/s, with capacitance retention of 91.3% after 5000 cycles. Li et al. [237] demonstrated SWCNTs/MnO2 nanosheet hybrids prepared by a wet-spinning method for wearable solid-state SCs. The portion of MnO2 in the hybrid was 75 wt% and intimate contact between MnO2 sheets and SWCNTs provided highly conductive pathways in the material, also enabling fast ion transport. The flexible solid-state SCs assembled from hybrid fibers exhibited volumetric capacitance of 74.8 F/cm3, energy density of 10.4 mWh/cm3 with good cycling stability. Jia et al. [238] reported mesostructured CNTs on vertically aligned MnO2 nanosheet composites for SCs. The unique structure of CNTs-on-MnO2 nanocomposites (MnO2: >90 wt %) exhibited specific capacity of 1131 F/g (based on the whole electrode) and 1229 F/g (based on MnO2) at a current density of 1 A/g, high rate capability, and stable cycling life (94.4%@10000 cycles). The authors have mentioned that vertically aligned MnO2 nanosheets shortened the ion diffusion paths, helping to improve the electrochemical performance. Lei et al. [239] reported fabrication of graphenated CNT/MnO2 nanowires composite on stainless steel substrates through CVD and electrodeposition method for binder-free SCs. The graphenated CNT/MnO2 nanowires composite demonstrated an areal capacitance of 492.5 mF/cm2 (current density: 0.5 mA/cm2) and energy density of 985 Wh/m2 with power density of 2.98 kW/m2. Capacitance retention was 95% after 5000 cycles at a current density of 0.5 mA/cm2. Wang et al. [240] demonstrated fabrication of 10 μm thin flexible CNT-MnO2 nanosheet composite films which showed good performance for SCs. The CNT film covered by MnO2 nanosheets was obtained via in-situ hydrothermal reaction. Narrow strips of CNT-MnO2 electrode were scrolled and twisted together with a negative electrode fabricated by synthesizing FeSe2 nanonuts on a CF to construct a novel coaxial yarn asymmetric SC. The fabricated device showed energy density up to 27.14 Wh/kg at a power density of 571.3 W/kg, with capacitance retention of 80.3% after 8000 charge/discharge cycles. Capacitance was 91.8% of the original value after bending the device by an angle of 180° and 80.8% of the initial value after 1800 bending cycles.

Other kinds of metal oxides have been loaded on the surface of CNTs for possible improvements in SC performance. Lee et al. [241] investigated CNTs with Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) for hybrid SCs. The hybrid SCs fabricated with CNTs/LTO anodes and CNT/AC cathodes delivered performance improvements in discharge capacitance, rate capability and cyclic ability, as well as energy and power density. A relatively modest (3 wt%) CNT addition into LTO anodes was enough for obtaining increased performance. Shinde et al. [242] demonstrated direct growth of tungsten trioxide (WO3) on MWCNTs for negative electrodes in flexible all-solid-state asymmetric SCs. The electrode materials were prepared by first coating carbon cloth substrates with MWCNTs and then by a hydrothermal treatment to deposit WO3 nanorods on the coated cloth. The electrode materials exhibited specific capacitance of 429.6 F/g and capacity retention of 94.3% after 5000 cycles. The asymmetric SCs with MWCNT-WO3 (negative electrode) and MnO2 (positive electrode) showed specific capacitance of 145.6 F/g (at 2 mA/g) and specific energy of 39.63 Wh/kg at a specific power of 546 W/kg. The device exhibited long-term cycling stability (77% capacitance retention) over 10000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanotubes-transition metal layered double hydroxides
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs associated with different metal hydroxides have been intensely studied as electrode materials for SCs [243–259]. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are important layer-structured materials due to their high theoretical capacitance, low cost and environmental friendliness. Layered compounds, especially electrochemically active LDHs have drawn increasing attention as electrode materials for high-performance SC electrodes [259–268]. CNTs assembled with LDH materials with unique morphologies have attained further performance improvements [269–276]. In the family of LDHs (NiAl, NiFe, NiCo, NiMn, CoMn, CoAl etc.), NiAl is frequently combined with CNTs as electrode materials for SCs. Several authors have reported that NiAl/CNTs composite formation improves electrochemical performance. Wang et al. [270] synthesized hierarchical NiAl-LDH/MWCNTs/Ni foam using hydrothermal method as electrode materials for SCs. The MWCNT surfaces were covered by NiAl-LDH hexagonal platelets, so the electrode was loaded with 5.8 mg of LDH per cm2 of MWCNT/Ni foam surface. The hybrids revealed areal capacitance of 7.5 F/cm2, specific capacitance of 1293 F/g, and cycling stability of 83% after 1000 charge/discharge cycles. Li et al. [271] described the synthesis of NiAl-LDH nanosheets/CNT by a solution method and used the composites as electrode materials for SC. Electrochemical measurements showed that NiAl-LDH nanosheets/CNTs composite contained specific capacitance of 1500 F/g (at 1 A/g) with cycling stability of 70.3% retention (at 10 A/g) in 2 M KOH electrolyte. The assembled asymmetric SCs devices (positive electrode: NiAl-LDHs/CNTs; negative electrode: AC) displayed specific capacitance of 115 F/g and energy density of 52 Wh/kg at 1 A/g. A precipitation approach for the synthesis of CNTs/NiAl-LDH based nanocomposite for SCs has been used by Bai et al. [272]. The NiAl-LDH/CNTs material exhibited specific capacitance of 694 F/g at 1A/g, keeping 87% of this value when the current density was increased to 10A/g. After 3000 charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 20 A/g the composite retained 92% of its original capacitance. Zhang et al. [273] reported hierarchical ultrathin NiAl-LDH nanosheet arrays on CNT paper as electrodes for hybrid SCs. The assembled asymmetric hybrid SCs (NiAl-LDH-CNTs//porous graphene nanosheets) displayed energy density of 50 Wh/kg at an average power density of 467 W/kg. Capacitance retention was 78% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles at 5 A/g. Wang et al. [274] demonstrated synthesis of core-shell structured NiAl-LDH/CNT composites using a hydrothermal method (Fig. 11 a) and studied its electrochemical performance for SCs. The NiAl-LDH/CNTs electrode (mass loading: 50 mg/cm2) showed specific capacity of 1017 C/g (areal capacity 50.85 C/cm2) at a current density of 1 A/g, with 85% of this value remaining when the current density was increased to 10 A/g (Fig. 11b). The specific capacity was increased by 6% after 50 test cycles, decreasing to 88% of the original value after 1000 cycles (Fig. 11c).

NiCo-LDH containing high theoretical capacitance (∼3000 F/g), has been considered as a promising electrode material for pseudo-SCs, with the electrochemical analysis indicating that both Ni2+ and Co2+ are redox-active [275,276]. Chen et al. [277] demonstrated facile assembly of NiCo LDH nanoflakes on CNTs network via chemical precipitation method for pseudo-SCs. In the synthesized hierarchical nanostructures, NiCo-LDH nanoflakes are arranged around large-diameter CNTs (diameter >50 nm). The optimized NiCo-LDH/CNT composite nanostructures exhibited electrochemical capacitance up to 1843 F/g at current density of 0.5 A/g and capacitance was maintained at 1231 F/g when the current density was increased to 10 A/g. The hierarchical nanostructures contained energy density of 51 W h/kg at a power density of 3.3 kW/kg. Ran et al. [278] reported controlled synthesis of carboxylated-CNTs connected with hollow NiCo-LDH via sequential ion exchange of carboxylated-CNTs-tailored MOF (ZIF-67) nanoarchitectures for charge storage. Due to the incorporation of carboxylated-CNTs, the derived NiCo-LDH hollow nanohybrids display high specific surface area, which plays a crucial role in the electrochemical reactions between the electrolyte ions and electrodes. The SCs assembled with NiCo-LDH/carboxylated-CNTs nanohybrids exhibited specific capacity of 855 C/g (at 1 A/g) and 778 C/g at 15 A/g, with energy density of 49.9 Wh/kg at a power density of 895 W/kg. Zhou et al. [279] suggested the formation of CNTs loaded with hollow-structured NiCo hydroxide for electrode materials in asymmetric SCs (Fig. 12 a and b; TEM images). The material was obtained by epitaxial growth based on the construction of hard template and a subsequent etching treatment. The composite electrode exhibits enhancement in structural stability and shows specific capacitance of 1896 F/g and 1479 F/g at 1 A/g and 40 A/g, respectively (Fig. 12c). The asymmetric SCs consisting of CNTs/NiCo hydroxide composite shows specific capacitance of 139.3 F/g at 1 A/g (Fig. 12d), energy density of 37.9 Wh/kg and a power density of 7005 W/kg (Fig. 12e). The asymmetric SCs maintain 91.6% of its initial specific capacitance and a coulombic efficiency close to 100% after 12500 cycles at 6 A/g (Fig. 12f). Many other research groups have been working on the synthesis of CNTs-LDHs for electrode materials in SC. Table 1 summarizes the most significant results obtained by some of these authors.

Transition metal sulfides are currently been evaluated as promising electrode materials for SCs due to their favorable physical and chemical properties, as well as high specific capacitance. Sulfides from metals such as iron, cobalt, nickel, copper, tin, manganese and molybdenum have been associated with 1D carbon materials for SC electrodes [283].

Many of the reported SC electrodes including metal oxides/hydroxides exhibit relatively poor rate capability. On the other hand, sulfides such as Co3S4 [284–287], Ni3S2 [288–291], CuS [292,293], ZnS [294–296] and MoS2 [291,297–300] have been reported to achieve improved electrochemical performance. Sun et al. [301] prepared uniform MoS2 nanolayers with sulfur vacancies (MoS2−x) on CNT networks as electrodes for SCs. The as-fabricated defect-rich MoS2@CNTs/Ni core/shell-structure used as electrode delivered a capacitance of 512F/g (at 1 A/g), keeping 342F/g at 30 A/g. and long cycle life without decay in retention for 2000 cycles. The fabricated asymmetrical SCs with MoS2-x@CNTs/Ni as the positive electrode and CNTs network as the negative electrode exhibited an energy density of 63 Wh/kg (at a power density of 850 W/kg) and kept 44.2 Wh/kg at 25.5 kW/kg. Li et al. [302] claimed that a mixed transition metal sulfide, CuCo2S4, associated with CNTs, can be a promising electrode material for asymmetric SCs (Fig. 13 a). The CNTs/CuCo2S4 composites were synthesized by hydrothermal and sulfuration process, with the CNTs embedded in a CuCo2S4 matrix. Fig. 13b shows the electrochemical analysis of CV curves and in Fig. 13c are the charge/discharge profiles of CNTs/CuCo2S4-3.2% electrode. As shown in Fig. 13d, when tested at 1 A/g, the composite electrode exhibited increased specific capacitance (557.5 F/g) when compared with a pristine CuCo2S4 electrode (373.4 F/g). The fabricated asymmetric SCs using CuCo2S4/CNTs-3.2% (positive electrode) and AC (negative electrode), showed an energy density of 23.2 Wh/kg at a power density of 402.7 W/kg. The specific capacitance of the device remained at 85.7% of its original value after being tested for 10000 cycles, indicating good cycling stability (Fig. 13e).

Ma et al. [303] described formation of CNT/Ni3S2 composites on the surface of Ni foam by electrodeposition. The electrode material contained large surface area and high capacitance (1643 F/g at 1 A/g and 999 F/g at 20 A/g). The Ni3S2/CNTs electrode shows good cycling stability, with capacitance retention of 91.5% after 2000 cycles. Wang et al. [304] designed network nanostructured electrodes composed of Ni3S2 nanoparticles and CNTs on a Ni foam substrate through electrodeposition combined with annealing. The Ni3S2/CNTs composites exhibited areal specific capacitance of 13.4 F/cm2 (at 10 mA/cm2), keeping 96.04% of this value at 50 mA/cm2. Asymmetric SCs assembled with Ni3S2/CNT (positive electrode) and AC (negative electrode) delivered energy density of 75.2 Wh/kg (power density: 2416 W/kg), areal capacitance of 425 mF/cm2 (3 mA/cm2) and capacitance retention of 90.6% after 10000 cycles.

The formation of composites combining CNTs and various polymers, such as PPy, PANI and PTh have been investigated for SCs applications. The electrodes are predominantly prepared by polymer coating/grafting on the surface of CNTs via chemical oxidative polymerization, interfacial polymerization, electrochemical polymerization or layer-by-layer assembly [305]. Chang et al. [306] fabricated core-shell structured pseudocapacitive anodes through electrochemical deposition of PPy on electrochemically pretreated CNT films. The CNTs/PPy electrode retain 75.2% of its capacitance (965.3 mF/cm2 at 1 mA/cm2) when the discharge current increased to 40 mA/cm2. After 10000 charge/discharge cycles, CNTs/PPy maintained 89.1% of its initial capacitance. The asymmetric SCs assembled by using CNTs/PPy and CNTs/MnO2 as anode and cathode, respectively, showed volumetric energy density of 3.63 mWh/cm3 at the power density of 13.86 mW/cm3 with 89.0% retention after 10000 charge/discharge cycles. Aydinli et al. [307] synthesized vertically aligned CNTs combined with PANI for SCs electrodes. The vertically aligned CNTs grown by CVD on Al foils were covered with a PANI layer by electrodeposition. The nanocomposite electrodes (obtained after 15 electrodeposition cycles) show specific capacitance of 16.17 mF/cm2 at a current density of 0.25 mA/cm2. Che et al. [308] have used 30 wt% CNTs together with PANI for porous nanocomposites (PANI/CNT30%). The PANI was chemically grafted on CNTs, and interpenetrating pores were generated via templating using CaCO3 nanoparticles (Fig. 14 a). The materials were evaluated as electrodes for SCs (Fig. 14b. CV curve; Fig. 14c charge/discharge curve). In PANI/CNT30% nanocomposite, the porous structure provides pathways for the transport of electrolyte ions inside the material. The composite exhibited a capacitance of 1266 F/g (at 1 A/g) and 864 F/g at 128 A/g (Fig. 14d). An 83% capacitance retention after 10000 continuous cycles has been measured (Fig. 14e).

Different kinds of conducting polymers have also been combined with CNTs for improving SC performances. Wang et al. [309] prepared N-doped porous carbon-CNTs composite SC electrodes derived from ZIF-8/PPy nanotubes. The SCs delivered specific capacitance of 248 F/g and 97.3% of capacitance retention after 3000 cycles. The capacitive performance was attributed to the conductive network for electron transport provided by the CNTs and the abundant sites for charge storage in the ZIF-derived porous carbon. Liu et al. [270] reported high performance all-solid-state flexible SCs based on a 3D morphology: CNT fibers/CNTs/PANI, with a double core-sheathed structure. The all-solid-state SCs displayed specific capacitance of 67.31 mF/cm2 at the current density of 0.5 mA/cm2, losing 10% of its initial specific capacitance after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. The SCs were able to withstand 180° bending deformation, maintaining 99.8% capacitance after 500 bending cycles. Potphode et al. [310] fabricated all-solid-state high energy density symmetric SCs based on partially exfoliated MWCNT/PANI composites with a redox-active polymer gel working as an electrolyte/separator. The authors compared the specific capacitances of symmetric SC cells made with MWCNTs (186.1 F/g), PANI (644.4 F/g) and the MWCNTs/PANI composite (809.6 F/g), with all values recorded at 25 mV/s. The polymer grafted morphology of composite in 1:1 wt ratio as an electrode material with redox-active gel polymer electrolyte revealed a synergistic effect in specific capacitance improvement, with higher stability (78% capacitive retention after 2000 cycles) than pure PANI (69.4% capacitive retention after 2000 cycles).

The numbers of published articles shows that PANI modified with CNTs are being intensively researched for use as electrode material in SCs. Some of the previous work reporting electrochemical measurements in PANI/CNT composite SC electrodes is summarized in Table 2 .

Heteroatom-doped 1D carbon materials have aroused widespread interest in the field of SCs due to their effective ion-accessible surface with low manufacturing cost [326–338]. Doping induces defects in the tubular lattice of 1D carbon materials, increasing surface chemical reactivity [339,340]. Due to the relatively easy doping with nitrogen, the N-doped CNTs play a leading role in the enhancement of electrochemical performance of SCs [341–343]. Liu et al. [344] reported cotton fabric and ZIF-8 derived porous N-doped CNTs/carbon fabric electrodes for all-solid-state devices. The flexible symmetric SCs revealed specific capacitance of 390 F/g (2 mV/s) and energy density of 23.4 Wh/kg at a power density of 91.4 W/kg. The devices displayed long-term cycling stability as well as good electrochemical performance under mechanical deformation. Zhu et al. [345] suggested an easy method for the synthesis of N-doped CNT/MnO2 composites prepared from PPy-derived N-doped CNTs containing MnO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 15 a) for SCs. The N-doped CNT/MnO2 electrodes showed specific capacitance of 366.5 F/g (at 0.5 A/g) and maintained 67% of this value at high current density (25 A/g), indicating good rate capability (Fig. 15b). The fabricated asymmetric SCs using N-doped CNTs/MnO2 and N-doped CNTs as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively, displayed energy density of 20.9 Wh/kg (Fig. 15c). Cycling stability was good, with 91.6% retention (coulombic efficiency: 98.8%) after 5000 cycles at 5 mA/cm2 (Fig. 15d). Ramesh et al. [346] demonstrated the controlled synthesis of N-doped CNTs/SnO2@NiCo2O4 hybrids. The synthesized electrodes showed specific capacitance of 728 F/g (at 4 A/g), with capacitance retention of 92% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles.

There are several kinds of CNFs; some of the widely accepted forms are platelet, fishbone, ribbon, stacked cup and thickened CNFs. These are categorized according to the structural arrangement of their graphitic planes [347,348]. CNFs possess superior mechanical and chemical properties, generating widespread interest in a wide range of applications, including catalysis, adsorption, structural reinforcement, nano-devices etc. Due to their low-cost, high electrical and thermal conductivities, large surface area and structural stability, CNFs have also been intensely investigated for use in electrochemical energy storage devices [349–354]. In SCs, CNFs play multi-functional roles as electrode materials with metal oxides, hydroxides, sulfides and polymers [355–363].

CNFs serving as conductive support matrices have been applied in energy storage devices due to their high electrical conductivity, mechanical stability, light weight and large scale synthesis at low cost [364–366]. Ma et al. [367] obtained flexible and free-standing CNF fabrics for SCs electrodes via electrospinning and nanocasting using phenolic resol as a carbon precursor and partially hydrolyzed tetraethyl orthosilicate as template. The CNF fabric electrodes delivered specific capacitance of 274 F/g at 0.1 A/g in H2SO4 electrolyte and 220 F/g at 0.5 A/g in solid-state SCs. Li et al. [368] demonstrated flexible micro/mesoporous CNFs embedded into ordered carbon prepared by coaxial electrospinning and subsequent corrosion. The specific capacitance of the material was 272 F/g (1 A/g), higher than that of CNFs (216 F/g). Symmetrical SCs based on these electrodes exhibited capacitance retention of 96.7% after 3000 charge/discharge cycles and energy density of 11.1 Wh/kg with power density of 0.25 kW/kg. Specific capacitance was not affected by 135° device bending. Ma et al. [369] investigated CNFs with multilevel gradient porous structure derived from PVDF/PVP core-shell nanofibers. The material revealed specific capacitance of 331 F/g (at 1 A/g) and capacitance retention of 99.6% after 10000 charge/discharge cycles (at 5 A/g). All-solid-state symmetric SCs assembled using the porous CNFs exhibited specific capacitance of 147.4F/g (1 A/g) with energy density of 13.1 Wh/kg and capacitance retention of 89.2% after 2000 charge/discharge cycles (at 5 A/g). Chang et al. [370] fabricated ultra-thin CNFs (28 ± 11 nm diameters) by electrospinning using polyacrylonitrile PAN/PMMA polymer fibers, followed by carbonization. The electrochemical analysis showed maximum specific capacitance of 243 F/g at 1 A/g and capacitance retention of 77.1% at a charge/discharge rate of 100 A/g. Bhoyate et al. [371] demonstrated PAN/polystyrene 1:1 wt ratio linear tube CNFs (LTCNFs) carbonized at 800 °C for SCs (Fig. 16 a). Surface engineered linear tube CNFs contains multiple porous tubes as shown in the SEM image (Fig. 16b). The LTCNFs revealed specific capacitance of 188 F/g at 0.5 A/g (CNF: 93 F/g) (Fig. 16c), with nearly 100% retention in charge storage capacity up to 5000 cycles (Fig. 16d). The fabricated symmetrical SCs device using LTCNFs delivered energy and power density of 30.5 Wh/kg and 8.3 kW/kg, respectively (Fig. 16e). Charge storage capacity improved by 150% when the temperature was increased from 10 to 60 °C.

Fabrication of electrodes for SCs using CNFs derived from waste coal described by Tan et al. [372]. First, depolymerized coal (coal char) was dissolved in DMF, and then the mixture was combined with polymeric precursors (PAN/PVP) to form a homogeneous solution for electrospinning and carbonization. The coal-derived CNFs revealed specific capacitance of 299.4 F/g (1 A/g) with capacitance retention of 93% after 10000 charge/discharge cycles. Ma et al. [373] prepared lignin-based porous CNFs films by electrospinning with PVP as spinning agent and Mg(NO3)2·6H2O as additive for SC electrodes. With the increase in Mg(NO3)2·6H2O content, the capacitive performance of lignin-based porous CNFs increased. A 2:1 mass ratio of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O: lignin demonstrated specific capacitance of 248 F/g (0.2 A/g) which was higher than that of CNF without Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (173 F/g). The lignin-based porous CNFs showed good rate performance (capacitance retention: 59–67% from 0.2 to 20 A/g) and cycling stability (capacitance retention: 97% after 1000 cycles at 20 A/g).

Metal/metal oxides/hydroxides have been used with CNFs as electrode materials for energy storage devices [374,375]. Tian et al. [376] suggested W2C nanoparticle-embedded CNF/CNT/GNR composites by electrospinning and carbonization for SC electrodes. The distribution of W2C nanoparticles formed in-situ during carbonization enhanced the electrode conductivity. The composite membrane exhibited specific capacitance of 256 F/g (1 A/g) and cycling stability with 95.6% retention after 2500 cycles. Kim et al. [377] tested free-standing and flexible Ag-plated CNF composites as electrode materials for SC applications. The material exhibited areal capacitance of 31 mF/cm2, energy density of 61 mWh/m2 and power density of 1.2 W/m2, with capacitance retention of ∼100% after 10000 charging/discharging cycles. Rate capability of 87% was obtained when the current density varied from 0.1 to 0.5 mA/cm2. Xu et al. [378] studied CNFs with NiS nanoparticles through electrospinning and calcination as a electrode materials for asymmetric SCs (Fig. 17 a and b). The CNFs-NiS electrode shows specific capacity of 177.1 mAh/g at 1 A/g (0.41 mAh/cm2 at a current density of 2.3 mA/cm2) and 88.7% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles (Fig. 17c and d). Asymmetric SCs with CNFs-NiS/CNFs as electrodes revealed 89.5% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles and volumetric energy density of 13.32 mWh/cm3 at a volumetric power density of 180 mW/cm3 (Fig. 17e and f).

A hybrid material incorporating NiCo2S4 nanotubes grown on CNFs by a hydrothermal process has been synthesized by Liu et al. [379] for high performance flexible SCs. The CNFs/NiCo2S4 nanotube hybrids exhibited specific capacitance of 360 F/g (0.3 A/g), with 90.0% retention after 2000 cycles. The assembled symmetric hybrid SCs also exhibited excellent flexibility. In a different study, Liu et al. [380] reported the decoration of CNFs with NiCo2S4 nanoparticles. The material was synthesized by a combination of electrospinning, carbonization and hydrothermal treatments for flexible asymmetric SCs. The hybrids display specific capacitance of 527.8 F/g and 622.5 F/g at current density of 0.2 A/g and scan rate of 2 mV/s respectively. Capacitance retention of 90.0% of the original value after 3000 cycles has also been reported. The asymmetric SCs based on CNFs/NiCo2S4 and porous CNFs coated with activated charcoal showed an energy density of 32.1 Wh/kg at a power density of 67.6 W/kg and 88.5% retention after 1500 cycles at 1.0 A/g. Dong et al. [381] reported synthesis of mesoporous CNFs using polymer/block copolymer blends by electrospinning process and high temperature carbonization. PAN was used as precursor, and the block copolymer of polyacrylic acid-b-PAN-b-polyacrylic acid was used as pore forming additive. The mesoporous CNFs display specific capacitance of 256.3 F/g when the amount of the block copolymer was 0.6 g. The symmetrical SCs assembled with mesoporous CNFs exhibited specific capacitance of 22.34 F/g and a capacitance retention of 90% after 80000 cycles. Mukhiya et al. [382] demonstrated synthesis of nanohaired 3D Co(OH)2 wheels/CNFs composite by electrospinning and hydrothermal method. The as-prepared material exhibited specific capacitance of 1186 F/g (at 1 A/g) with good cyclic stability. Asymmetric SC devices using 3D Co(OH)2/CNFs as a positive electrode and N-doped graphene hydrogel as a negative electrode exhibited high energy density of 60.31 Wh/kg and 37 Wh/kg at power densities of 740.8 W/kg and 7500 W/kg, respectively. Pech et al. [383] studied the electrochemical performances of CNFs, interconnected CNFs, and CNFs/MnOx composites synthesized by conventional and core-shell electrospinning techniques, combined with heat treatments and CO2 activation. The composite electrode displayed higher electrochemical capacitance when compared to conventional CNFs and interconnected CNFs. The CNFs/MnOx composite showed specific capacitance of 213.7 F/g at the current density of 0.5 A/g, energy density of 30 mWh/g at the power density of 249 mW/g, and cycling stability of 97% after 1000 cycles.

Organic conducting polymers such as PANI, PPy, etc. polymerized on CNF frameworks with different architectures have been intensely studied for application in SC electrodes [384–392]. Luo et al. [393] synthesized freestanding CNFs containing helical CNTs with PANI. Helical CNTs were grown on the CFs by CVD and then combined with PANI by in-situ polymerization to form a 3D porous structure. The flexible SC electrodes show capacitance of 660 F/g (at 1 A/g) with good cycling stability (90.4% capacitance retention after 1000 charge/discharge cycles). The electrochemical performance did not suffer significant effects after bending and twisting, showing 95.4% capacitance retention after 500 bending/recovery cycles. Yanilmaz et al. [394] fabricated flexible PANI/CNF composites and evaluated their electrochemical performance for symmetric SCs. Sol-gel and electrospinning methods were used for flexible CNFs. PANI coatings were applied via in-situ chemical polymerization. The binder-free flexible PANI/CNF electrodes revealed specific capacitance of 234 F/g with capacitance retention of 90% after 1000 cycles. The electrode prepared with 12 h polymerization showed an energy density of 32 Wh/kg at the power density of 500 W/kg. The growth of PPy on coiled CNFs via electropolymerization has been suggested by Dumanli et al. [395] for SC electrode materials. In CNF/PPy composite formation, depositions of PPy on CNFs were optimized by varying the degree of deposition and deposition speed. The coiled CNF/PPy composite revealed capacitance of 27.6 C/cm2 at 6th cycling of CV using scan rate of 25 mV/s. Jiang et al. [396] fabricated flexible freestanding electrodes using interconnected chitin nanofibers, MWCNTs and milled CNFs. The conductive substrate was decorated with PPy to improve the capacitance. The chitin nanofibers/MWCNTs/CNFs/PPy films reached a specific capacitance of 362 F/g (5 mV/s), with retention of 86.6% after 5000 cycles at 2 A/g. Abdah et al. [397] studied CNFs/PPy/MnO2 composites as electrode materials for SCs. The functionalized composite was synthesized in a process with multiple steps (electrospinning, carbonization, electrochemical functionalization, in-situ chemical polymerization and electrodeposition), as shown in Fig. 18 a. The functionalized CNFs/PPy/MnO2 composite showed specific capacitance of 409.88 F/g (Fig. 18b), which was higher than both CNFs/MnO2 (322.96 F/g) and CNFs/PPy (290.83 F/g). Symmetric functionalized CNFs/PPy/MnO2 SCs displayed specific energy of 42.53 Wh/kg at a specific power of 297.32 W/kg, with capacitance retention of 86.3% over 3000 cycles (Fig. 18c and d).

Using a combined solution of MnCl2, B2O3, PAN, and pitch, Yang et al. [398] reported the synthesis (by electrospinning) of composite SC electrodes containing MnO2 and boron-incorporated CNFs. The composite material exhibited high specific surface area, mesoporous structure, and boron and MnO2 functional groups as electroactive sites. The authors have suggested that boron and MnO2 functional groups provide low internal resistance for charge diffusion due to the improved wettability of electrolyte ions into the pores and the increased number of redox sites. These effects led to high gravimetric capacitance and energy density with enhanced cycling stability. Cai et al. [399] suggested the synthesis of MnCo2O4@N-doped CNF composites by carbonization of manganese-cobalt@PAN nanofibers under N2 atmosphere for symmetric SC electrodes. Symmetric SCs based on the composite electrode displayed specific capacitance of 871.5 F/g (0.5 A/g) and energy density of 30.26 Wh/kg, with retention of 89.3% capacitance after 5000 cycles. Dahal et al. [356] reported the fabrication of B/N co-doped 3D porous CNFs network incorporating MOF (ZIF-8) nanoparticles as a binder-free electrode for SCs. The electrospinning of PAN and ZIF-8 nanoparticles was used to fabricate the porous CNFs, followed by a sodium borohydride treatment, freeze-drying and carbonization. The electrode delivered specific capacitance of 295 F/g at 0.5 A/g current density, with cyclic stability of 94.5% capacitance retention after 10000 charge/discharge cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon yarn (carbon nanotubes/carbon nanofibers) modified composites
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon yarns using CNTs/CNFs can be processed in several ways, but the scientific literature provides guidelines for preparing high-strength CNTs/CNFs yarns [400–404] for flexible SCs. Recently, there has been remarkable progress in the fabrication of carbon-based yarns using CNTs/CNFs for high performance SC electrode materials [398,405–410].

CNT yarns made from twisted CNTs into the form of sheets show excellent mechanical and electrical properties, high surface area and light weight, thus achieving high electrochemical performance [411–414]. Smithyman et al. [415] designed flexible SCs using CNT yarns. The coaxial yarn cell design revealed flexural deformation with negligible impact on the energy storage performance. Shang et al. [416] described stretchable SCs based on helical CNT yarns with elastic polymeric substrates. The helical CNT yarn SCs showed stable performance under high deformation conditions (tensile strain up to 150%), and cyclic stretching (up to 10 Hz over 10000 cycles). Researchers have suggested the fabrication of two-ply yarn SCs using CNTs [417–419]. Su et al. [417] suggested fabrication of flexible two-ply yarn SCs based on gamma irradiated CNT yarns with PEDOT/PSS. Gamma irradiated CNT yarns showed improved electrical conductivity as well as capacitance/gram of the as-spun CNT two-ply yarn SCs. PEDOT/PSS coating on the surface of the pure and gamma irradiated CNT yarns improved the capacitance of the SCs. Su et al. [418] reported two-ply yarn SCs fabrication by electrodeposition of transition metal oxide pseudocapacitive materials on CNTs/stainless steel core spun yarns. The stainless steel core incorporated in the CNTs yarn improves the conductivity of substrate and the efficiency of electrodeposition of metal oxides on the electrodes. Both MnOx and Co3O4, when electrodeposited on spun CNT/stainless steel yarn shows improved SC performance. The solid-state SCs based on MnOx/CNT/stainless steel composite yarn shows better electrochemical properties than Co3O4/CNT/stainless steel composite yarn. The MnOx/CNT/stainless steel composite yarn contains specific capacitance of 217.61 F/cm3 (0.02 V/s) and energy density of 4.84 mWh/cm3 (power density: 435.22 mW/cm3). Lyu et al. [419] obtained two-ply yarn SCs based on CNTs/stainless steel core-sheath yarn electrodes. The devices were flexible and strong enough for use as sewing threads and for making knots (Fig. 19 a). The authors have suggested that the core-sheath yarn structure improves charge transport efficiency, allowing the length of the linear SCs to be significantly scaled up. Fig. 19b compares the dependence of volumetric capacitance on SC length for CNT and CNT/stainless steel yarns at the scan rate of 0.5 V/s in ionic liquid electrolyte. The electrochemical performances of SCs under different scanning rates are shown in Fig. 19c. The specific capacitance loss after 1000 charge/discharge cycles was 8.1% for CNT/stainless steel core-sheath yarn electrodes and 13.4% for CNT yarns, as shown in Fig. 19d.

Fabrication of flexible SCs using CNT yarns and MnO2 has been described by Choi et al. [420]. The CNTs yarns contain MnO2 trapped in the internal pores, effectively increasing electrochemical area and reducing charge diffusion length. The specific capacitance of 25.4 F/cm3 (10 mV/s) and energy of 3.52 mWh/cm3 with power density of 127 mW/cm3 were obtained from micrometer-diameter all-solid-state yarn SCs. Choi et al. [421] reported fabrication of asymmetrically configured CNT yarn-based SCs using a liquid-state bi-scrolling technology. High loading amounts of guest materials (rGO anode: 90.1 wt%; MnO2 cathode: 70 wt%) were embedded into CNTs yarn host electrodes. The rGO embedded CNT yarn electrode revealed charge storage capability of 172 mF/cm2. The MnO2 embedded CNT yarn shows wide voltage windows up to 2.1 V (aqueous electrolyte) and 3.5 V (organic electrolyte). The areal energy densities measured for all-solid-state asymmetric SCs were 30.1 μW h/cm2 and 43 μW h/cm2 for PVA-LiCl and PVDF-HFP-TEA·BF4 gel electrolytes, respectively.

A spinning-based process to fabricate continuous nanofiber yarns for application in SCs has been described by Zhuang et al. [422]. The nanofiber yarns shows specific capacitance of 15.8 F/g at the current density of 2 A/g and cycling stability with 8.8% capacitance loss after 1200 cycles (current density: 2 A/g). Zhai et al. [423] obtained all-carbon solid-state yarn SCs using commercially available AC and CF yarns. A 10 cm long yarn SCs, with the optimum composition of 2.2 mg/cm AC and 1 mg/cm CF displays a specific length capacitance of 45.2 mF/cm (2 mV/s), an energy density of 6.5 μWh/cm with power density of 27.5 μW/cm. Also, the SCs exhibited good mechanical flexibility with minor capacitance decreases upon bending or crumpling. Mao et al. [424] demonstrated CF yarns coated with aligned CNFs modified with PANI nanowire array. The assembled solid-state SCs show areal specific capacitance of 234 mF/cm2 (0.1 mA/cm2) and energy density of 21.4 μWh/cm2 (power density: 0.52 mW/cm2). The SCs exhibited capacitance retention of 90% after 8000 cycles. Khalid et al. [425] fabricated bendable tube-shaped SCs based on rGO and Prussian blue-coated CF yarns electrodes via electrochemical process. The PB coated CF yarns and rGO coated CF yarns exhibited specific capacitance of 339 F/g and 160.2 F/g, respectively. The electrodes coupled inside a flexible plastic tube (separated by the electrolyte wet filter paper) carried specific energies of 52.1 Wh/kg and 26.5 Wh/kg with specific powers of 3100 W/kg and 14400 W/kg, respectively. Chen et al. [426] synthesized core-shell structured SC morphology using PAN-based CNF yarn with PPy and rGO for all-solid-state fiber SCs. The flexible SCs revealed specific capacitance of 92.57 F/g at the scan rate of 2 mV/s and 86% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles.

Carbon yarns coated with ordered macroporous NiFe2O4 have been studied by Zhao et al. [427] for knittable fibriform SCs. The SCs display mechanical flexibility, with 12% capacitance drop after 1200 bending cycles and good wearability. The assembled fibriform SCs exhibited specific capacitance of 247 mF/cm (scan rate: 10 mV/s) and cycling stability with 96% capacitance retention after 8000 cycles. Wei et al. [428] prepared cotton yarn electrodes coated with PPy nanotubes for all-solid-state yarn SCs. The network structured PPy increased the electrode/electrolyte interface area, improving electrochemical performance. The proposed all-solid-state yarn SCs contained specific capacitance of 74.0 mF/cm2 and energy density of 7.5 μWh/cm2. Ullah et al. [429] demonstrated self-standing cotton yarn-derived CF with MnO2 electrodes. The materials exhibited areal specific capacitance of 182.94 mF/m2 at 20 mV/s with high energy and power densities (16.88 μWh/cm2 and 4770 μW/cm2, respectively). Jin et al. [430] described the synthesis of cotton yarn fibers containing graphene/PANI composite, as shown in Fig. 20 a. The material was composed by fiber cores inside cotton yarns with graphene sheets and a PANI nanowire array layer grown through in-situ polymerization of aniline. The authors have suggested that PANI nanowires ensure highly active electrochemical surface area, with spaces within the PANI nanowire array layer and hierarchical pores of yarn electrode favoring fast electrolyte ion diffusion. The material showed areal capacitance of 246 mF/cm2 (5 mV/s) and capacitance retention of 98% after 3800 cycles (Fig. 20b and c). Symmetric all-solid-state yarn SCs carried an energy density of 9.7 μWh/cm2 at a power density of 840.9 μW/cm2 (Fig. 20d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D carbon-based materials for supercapacitors: Graphene derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and its porous derivatives offer great advantages in electrode materials for SCs. Some of its more important characteristics are high surface area, enhanced ion/electron transport, improved material loading per unit area of substrate and enhanced mechanical flexibility/stability during repetitive charge/discharge. Graphene and its derivatives provide high energy storage capacity when compared to regular carbon-based materials [431–433]. For further improvement in the electrochemical performance of graphene-based materials for SCs, heteroatom doping/co-doping is a widely adopted strategy [434–436].

Graphene-based materials are well suited as electrodes for SCs [437–450]. Xing et al. [451] reported a cost-effective approach to obtain high surface area graphene nanosheets from humic acid via carbonization coupled with oxidation-exfoliation-thermal reduction. The SC electrode exhibited specific capacitance of 272 F/g (at 50 mA/g), retaining 96.5% of the initial capacitance after 8000 charge/discharge cycles.
