The SCs exhibited energy density of 6.47 Wh/kg (power density: 2250 W/kg). Singh et al. [452] obtained graphene nanosheets for flexible SCs by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite. The authors analyzed the electrochemical behavior of SCs in seven different kinds of gel electrolytes. They found that PVA/KOH gel electrolyte shows maximum specific capacitance for their SCs. Silva et al. [453] obtained graphene sheets by unzipping CNTs via microwave-CVD in H2 atmosphere. The material yielded specific energy of 4.8 W h/kg at a current density of 4 A/g. Xie et al. [454] used graphene nanosheets with conductive POM to fabricate graphene-based SCs. The authors found that when compared to pristine graphene nanosheets, dispersion of POM into graphene nanosheets significantly improves the cycling stability of POM/graphene nanosheets.

The direct laser micro-patterning and reduction of GO, a lithography-free method, is frequently used to design in-plane SCs/micro-SCs [455–463]. Yang et al. [464] synthesized graphene by excimer laser reduction (with different laser powers and times) of graphite oxide suspended in aqueous media. The fabricated graphene contains randomly aggregated, crumpled, disordered and small size solid material. The specific capacitance of the laser fabricated graphene depended on the energy and irradiation time. The highest specific capacitances measured were 141 F/g (1.04 A/g) and 84 F/g (1.46 A/g) in aqueous and CH3CN electrolytes, respectively. Li et al. [465] described in-plane flexible micro-SCs supported onto photographic paper using in-situ femtosecond laser-reduced GO/Au nanoparticles. The laser treatment reduced the hydrated GO and HAuCl4 simultaneously, incorporating both the patterning of rGO electrodes and the fabrication of Au current collectors in a single step, as shown in Fig. 21 a. The SEM image in Fig. 21b shows a laser-written rGO/Au micro-SC, with a triangle shaped cross-sectional structure of microelectrodes. The fabricated flexible micro-SCs offered rate capability of 50% as scan rate increased from 0.1 V/s to 100 V/s. Specific capacitances were 0.77 mF/cm2 (volumetric capacitance: 17.2 F/cm3) and 0.46 mF/cm2 (volumetric capacitance: 10.2 F/cm3) at scan rates of 1 V/s and 100 V/s, respectively (Fig. 21c).

Electrochemically exfoliated graphene for electrode materials in SCs have been reported by Tsai et al. [466]. The specific capacitance was 59 F/g and remained above 80% of the maximum value as the scan rate was increased from 5 mV/s to 1 V/s. The cyclic performance of an AC/electrochemically exfoliated graphene cell revealed long-term stability, with 95% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles. The authors have mentioned that electrochemically exfoliated graphene cells and AC/electrochemically exfoliated graphene (1:1 mass ratio) cells show rate capabilities that compared favorably with commercial high-power SCs. Augusto et al. [467] described development of flexible electrodes based on multilayer graphene paper for electrochemical SCs. The SCs were assembled from multilayer graphene electrodes and PANI-coated multilayer graphene. Capacitances of 58 mF/cm2 (areal capacitance) and 451 F/g (specific capacitance) were recorded, with 84% and 103% of retention after 1000 cycles, respectively. Energy and power density for the PANI/multilayer graphene SCs were 17 Wh/g and 3.61 kW/kg, respectively. Electrode materials based on thiolated-graphene using NaSH as a reducing agent for GO have been suggested by Kannappan et al. [468]. At a current density of 2.5 A/g, the thiolated-graphene SCs exhibited specific capacitance, power density and energy density of 195 F/g, 4.3 kW/kg and 83.4 Wh/kg, respectively. At a higher current density of 7.5 A/g, they still displayed specific capacitance of 137 F/g and capacitance retention of 98% after 10000 cycles. Sethi et al. [469] studied the synthesis of few layers porous graphene using an eco-friendly solvothermal method. The porous graphene electrode showed specific capacitance of 666 F/g (at 5 mV/s) with 87% retention of initial capacitance after 10000 cycles. The SCs also sustain an energy density of 26.3 Wh/kg with a power density of 6120 W/kg. Peng et al. [470] described the preparation of SC electrodes from high density graphene flakes. The material was made by shredding thermally reduced GO films into small pieces. The high density graphene flakes exhibited specific capacitance of 237 F/g and volumetric capacitance of 261 F/cm3 (at 0.1 A/g), with capacitance retention of 98% after 10000 cycles. Symmetrical SCs of high density graphene flakes exhibited gravimetric (volumetric) energy density of 15 Wh/kg (16 Wh/L) at a power density of 80 W/kg (88 W/L).

Synthesis of graphene fibers form macroscopic GO fibers obtained by dry spinning with high temperature airflow demonstrated by Zhang et al. [471]. After chemical reduction of GO fibers, the obtained graphene fibers show specific capacitance of 78.8 F/m2 at the current of 20 μA. Lu et al. [472] reduced the size of GO flakes from micrometers to a few nanometers via sonochemical treatment and used the material for SC electrodes. According to the structural and electrochemical analysis, the reduced size of graphene results in a disrupted aromatic structure on the edges with enriched sp3-carbon defects. The increased number of edges in small size graphene has no positive impact on its capacitance enhancement. Guardia et al. [473] demonstrated electrode materials for SCs combining rGO with microporous carbons. The composite electrodes with rGO have lower resistance, enabling higher rate capability. The mixture of rGO and particles of porous carbon (obtained by KOH activation) reached specific capacitance of 260 F/g (1 mA/cm2). This value increased 2.4 times for a current density of 200 mA/cm2. At high current densities, the composite electrodes stored eight times more energy and had four times higher power density. Bora et al. [474] fabricated flexible asymmetric SCs based on Ag and PANI functionalized rGO aerogels using a low temperature organic sol-gel method. In the asymmetric SCs, Ag nanorod-functionalized rGO aerogel is the negative electrode and PANI nanotube-functionalized rGO aerogel the positive electrode. The all-solid-state asymmetric SCs delivered energy density of 52.85 Wh/kg and power density of 31.5 kW/kg with good cycling and temperature stability.

Heteroatom doping in graphene and its derivatives usually improves the electrochemical performance for energy storage [475–482]. Authors have reported that heteroatom-doped graphene derivatives are valuable materials to modulate chemical, electronic, and structural properties in SCs due to their outstanding characteristics [483–489]. Doping enhances the electrochemical performance of carbon materials, mainly due to the following reasons:
1)
Defect creation in carbon materials upon doping makes the materials more porous, which enhances the accessibility of the electrolyte ions during electrochemical processes.
2)
Doping creates numerous active sites, increasing the reactivity of carbon materials.
3)
The electrical conductivity can be enhanced through the doping process.
4)
Heteroatom doping generates pseudocapacitance, in addition to the EDLC capacitance of the carbon materials.
5)
The doping process also enhances the wettability of the active carbon materials, favoring electrochemical reactions.

N-doped graphene sheets and applied them as electrode materials in SCs synthesized by Wang et al. [490]. Graphene doping was performed under hydrothermal conditions by using ammonium salts with hard acid-soft base pairs as N-doping agents. The electrochemical behavior of N-doped graphene sheets was evaluated in a symmetric two-electrode cell configuration with 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, exhibiting specific capacitance of 242 F/g (1 A/g). Shau et al. [491] reported the synthesis of N-doped graphene (15% nitrogen) using silk cocoon membranes for application in SC electrodes. By pyrolyzing the cocoon at 400 °C in argon atmosphere, the N-doped graphene enhanced SC performance. The authors reasoned that the enhanced performance was due to the high electronegativity of nitrogen which forms dipoles on the graphene surface, enhancing the tendency of graphene to attract charged species. Liu et al. [492] prepared N-doped graphene from graphite oxide powder using an hexamethylenetetramine flame. Hexamethylenetetramine acts as fuel for the flame as well as being a nitrogen source and reduction agent. The N-doped graphene contains 2.95 at% nitrogen in pyridinic, pyrrolic, quaternary and amino configurations. The material was used as electrode for SCs, revealing low internal resistance and good stability, with specific capacitance of 205.3 F/g in 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. Lin et al. [493] described the synthesis of N-doped graphene/carbon nanohorns composite by one-step co-pyrolysis of GO, carbon nanohorns, urea and lignosulfonate. The composite electrode material showed specific capacitance of 363 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g and 93.5% capacitance retention after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. Wang et al. [494] prepared N-doped graphene forests using plasma enhanced CVD (with C2H2 and NH3) (Fig. 22 a). When compared with non-doped graphene forests, specific area capacitance and volumetric capacitance of N-doped graphene forest-based SCs increased by approximately 26% and 89%, respectively (Fig. 22b and c). High cycling stability was achieved, with no degradation after 10000 cycles, while the energy density was improved by 87% (Fig. 22d and e). These flexible wearable SCs exhibited no capacitance decrease under dynamic bending tests.

Synthesis of pyridinic-rich N-doped graphene nanoplatelets using acid–catalyzed dehydration reaction between GO and 3,4-diaminopyridine demonstrated by Lee et al. [495]. Upon optimized N doping (N content: 5.3 wt%) graphene showed higher specific capacitance (214 F/g at a current density of 0.1 A/g). Wang et al. [436] reported chemoselective solution synthesis of pyrazolic-structure-rich N-doped graphene for SCs. The N-doped graphene SC with acidic hydrogel electrolytes provided a specific capacitance of 226 F/g and energy density of 30.3 Wh/kg. With neutral hydrogel electrolytes, pyrazolic-structure-rich N-doped graphene SC revealed an energy density of 35.8 Wh/kg. Balaji et al. [496] reported the synthesis of N-doped graphene using glycine as a N precursor through a supercritical fluid method. The N-doped graphene shows specific capacitance of 270 F/g (0.5 A/g) with high capacitance retention of 90% over 10000 cycles (at 10 A/g). The symmetric SC cells revealed energy densities of 4.1 and 36 Wh/kg in aqueous and ionic liquid electrolytes, respectively. Wu et al. [497] demonstrated the synthesis of N-doped few layer graphene from expandable graphite for SC applications. The N precursor, C3H6N6 reacted with graphene through a heating process. The electrodes showed areal capacitance of 83.8 mF/cm2 with high cycling stability. N-doped ordered mesoporous carbon/graphene composites have been described by Sui et al. [498]. SC electrodes were obtained by evaporation-induced self-assembly and low temperature carbonization. Phenolic resin (carbon source), triblock copolymer (F127) (template), hydrochloric acid (catalyst), tetraethylorthosilicate (structure enhancing agent) and urea (additive for improving graphitization and surface wettability) were used for synthesis of ordered mesoporous carbon. Graphene was added into the mixed solution with the carbon source during synthesis. The N-doped ordered mesoporous carbon/graphene composites showed specific capacitance of 246 F/g at 1 A/g with good stability. Dai et al. [499] described a facile and scalable synthesis of N-doped porous graphene for high performance SCs with specific capacitance of 390 F/g. Symmetric SC devices fabricated with the N-doped graphene electrodes showed energy density of 55 Wh/kg at a power density of 1800W/kg, with capacitance retention of 96.6% after 20000 cycles. Jin et al. [500] described free-standing porous N-doped graphene hydrogel films for SCs (Fig. 23 a). The authors have mentioned that N-doped structure and macro-pore formation (SEM image in Fig. 23b) ensured high pseudocapacitance and fast ion adsorption, respectively. The symmetric assembled SCs revealed specific capacitance of 455.4 F/g with long term cycling stability (104% of its initial capacitance) after 5000 cycles (Fig. 23c and d). Also, asymmetric SCs with N-doped graphene (negative electrode) and macro-porous graphene/PPy composite (positive electrode) showed specific capacitance of 86 F/g (1 A/g), energy density of 34.51 Wh/kg (power density: 849.77 W/kg) and cycling stability with 97.4% of capacitance retention over 5000 cycles at 5 A/g (Fig. 23e–g).

Predominant pyridinic N-doped rGO (6.2 at% N) monoliths using GO and melamine through an ice-template assembly have been fabricated by Kota et al. [501]. The synthesized materials showed specific capacitance values up to 217 F/g at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. This result was three times better than the one for undoped 3D rGO. The 3D N-rGO exhibited good cyclic stability, without any capacitance decay after 5000 cycles (at 100 mV/s).

Several researchers have reported that B-doping in graphene derivatives improves the performance of electrode materials used in SC applications. Thirumal et al. [502] described the formation of B-doped graphene nanosheets from GO and H3BO3 using a hydrothermal method. B-doped graphene electrodes exhibited twice the capacitance of pristine graphene nanosheets (113 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g). Balaji et al. [503] used GO and H3BO3 to synthesize B-doped GO by two different methods: supercritical fluid processing and hydrothermal route. The B-doped GO electrode synthesized from supercritical fluid processing exhibited specific capacitance of 286 F/g (1 A/g) in 20% KOH solution, which was higher than B-doped GO synthesized via hydrothermal method (142 F/g). The electrode showed specific capacitance retention of 96% (20 A/g) over 10000 cycles. Symmetric SC cells (from supercritical fluid processing) exhibited 84% and 67% of capacitance retention in 1 M NaClO4 and 20% KOH electrolytes, respectively. The energy density was 21.2 Wh/kg (1 M NaClO4 aqueous solution) which was four times higher when compared to 20% KOH solution (5.3 Wh/kg).

Sulfur doping in graphene also enhances the performance of SCs. Zhang et al. [504] suggested sulfur-doped graphene as electrode material for long-term cycling stability of SCs. The sulfur-doped graphene contains unique oxidized-S species, exhibiting capacitance retention up to 100.8% after 10000 charge/discharge cycles.

Co-doping/dual atom doping is a popular strategy to modify the electrochemical properties of graphene and its derivatives in order to improve the performance of SCs. Cheng et al. [505] reported radiolytic synthesis of N–S co-doped graphene for SCs. In their approach, an electron-beam (from electron accelerator) was used for reduction of GO as well as co-doping of N and S in graphene. l-cysteine has been used as the precursor for the N and S dopants. The dual doped graphene structure contained 4.88–10.88 at% N and 2.08–4.59 at% S. The electrode material irradiated with a dose of 210 kGy exhibited specific capacitance of 227.94 F/g (10 mV/s) with a capacitance retention of 83% after 25000 cycles at a current density of 10 A/g. Tian et al. [506] synthesized N–S co-doped graphene by heating graphene with (NH4)2SO4 and melamine in a sealed vacuum copper tube at 600 °C. Doping has taken place through the self-generated gas under high temperature and pressure (Fig. 24 a). The SC electrode material revealed a specific capacitance as 264.3 F/g at 0.5 A/g (Fig. 24b). Symmetric SCs exhibited good rate capability, with 82.4% capacitance preserved from 1 to 20 A/g. Cycling stability was characterized by 95.1% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles (at 5 A/g) (Fig. 24c and d).

N–P co-doped graphene using (NH4)3PO4 as doping precursor prepared by Xia et al. [507]. The authors used the material in SC electrodes, achieving specific capacitances of 219 F/g (123 F/cm3) at 0.25 A/g and 175 F/g (98 F/cm3) at 10 A/g. Also, the symmetrical SC devices provided energy density of 8.2 Wh/kg (4.6 Wh/L) at a power density of 162 W/kg (91 W/L), maintaining 6.7 Wh/kg at 6.5 kW/kg. The device kept 86% of the original capacitance after 20000 charge/discharge cycles (at 5 A/g). Nazarian-Samani et al. [508] described the fabrication of N–P doped holey graphene for SCs using glucose, dicyandiamide and H3PO4. The presence of H3PO4 acting as an “acid catalyst” encouraged the breaking of CC bonds to create localized perforations in graphene. The electrode material provided specific capacitance of 235.5 F/g (0.5 A/g), rate capability of 84.8% (from 0.5 to 70 A/g) and capacitance retention of 93.2% at 10 A/g over 25000 cycles. El-Gendy et al. [509] reported the synthesis of Na–N–P co-doped graphene nanosheets using guanosine monophosphate (green reagent) and NaOH for high-performance SCs. Sodium-N-P- co-doped graphene nanosheets revealed specific capacitance of 499 F/g (1 mV/s), cycling retention of 101% (at 0.2 V/s) after 1000 cycles and energy density of 98.58 Wh/kg. Yu et al. [510] obtained P–S co-doped graphene via an ice-template and thermal-activation approach for high performance flexible SCs. The flexible film devices exhibited pseudocapacitive behavior, with specific capacitance of 169 F/g (1A/g), rate capability of 91.7% (from 1A/g to 30A/g) and cycling stability of 92.5% after 2000 charge/discharge cycles. After bending at 120o, there was no change in specific capacitance, confirming the good characteristics for application in all-solid-state flexible SCs.

A holey N-doped graphene aerogel with high surface area (446 m2/g) and low charge transfer resistance has been synthesized by Xu et al. [511] for non-flammable, thermally stable electrodes in SCs. The synthesized aerogel displayed specific capacity of 318.3 F/g (0.5 A/g) in 6 M KOH electrolyte. The symmetrical SC revealed specific capacitance of 96.0 F/g (at 200 A/g). After 10000 cycles (at 2 A/g) specific capacitance was 262.5 F/g with capacitance retention of 98.4%. The device kept an energy density of 60.3 Wh/kg (power density: 0.9 kW/kg) in non-flammable ionic liquid. Du et al. [512] synthesized highly porous N-doped graphene aerogels using urea and KOH by drying at ambient pressure and temperature. The porous N-doped graphene aerogel was used as binder-free SC electrode, showing specific capacitance of 175 F/g (0.5 A/g) with 87% of initial capacity being retained after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. Li et al. [513] used mesoporous B-doped graphene aerogels synthesized via hydrothermal method for electrodes in aqueous symmetric SCs. B-doped graphene aerogels displayed specific capacitance of 308.3 F/g (1 A/g) and capacitance retention of 92% after 5000 cycles at 1A/g.

One-pot synthesis process for obtaining N–S co-doped porous graphene aerogels for SC electrodes suggested by Chen et al. [514]. The material was tested with an ionic liquid (EMIMBF4) electrolyte, offering specific capacitance of 203.2 F/g (1 A/g) and capacitance retention of 90% after 3000 cycles (at 2 A/g). The device also provided an energy density of 100.7 Wh/kg at a power density of 0.94 kW/kg. Zhang et al. [515] described the synthesis of N–S co-doped graphene hydrogels by hydrothermal processing using NH3 and CH4N2S as dopant sources (Fig. 25 a). The synthesized hydrogel N–S co-doped electrode material revealed high specific capacity of 1063 C/g (295.3 mAh/g) at a current density of 1 A/g. The specific capacity decreased with repeated charge/discharge cycles (at 10 A/g), remaining at 76% of its original value after 6000 cycles (Fig. 25b). The cycling performance of symmetric all-solid-state SCs assembled with the hydrogel and Ni foam (Fig. 25c) was characterized by a capacitance retention of 69% after 4000 cycles at a current density of 2 A/g (Fig. 25d). The SCs kept an energy density of 6.25 Wh/kg at a power density of 500W/kg.

The synthesis of N-doped porous rGO by a hydrothermal method using liquid NH3 and rGO at 200 °C (for 24 h) has been described by Singh et al. [516]. The resulting material was used as electrode in flexible all-solid-state SC devices. The N-doped porous rGO material showed specific capacitance of 230 F/g (1 A/g) and capacitance retention up to ∼88% after 10000 charge/discharge cycles at 5 A/g. The authors have mentioned that synergistic effects between the porosity and N-doping in rGO improved charge storage ability. Selvakumar et al. [517] pyrolyzed GO films under nitrogen atmosphere to form N-doped rGO. The freestanding flexible N-doped rGO films were applied as electrodes in symmetric SC devices. PVA/phosphoric acid was used as the separator for the solid-state SCs structure and the fabricated SC devices showed a specific capacitance of 397 F/g. Sliwak et al. [518] reported N-doped rGO (N content: 10.9–13.4 at%) synthesized by hydrothermal route in aqueous GO dispersion with C2H4N4. The highest nitrogen-containing N-rGO revealed specific capacitance of 244 F/g at the scan rate of 100 mV/s, exhibiting an increased rate capability when compared to undoped rGO. After 5000 charge/discharge cycles, 92% of the initial capacitance was retained due to the high stability of the electrochemically active nitrogen moieties.

A supramolecular strategy to synthesize N-doped (10.5 at.%) porous rGO for SC electrodes has been published by Cheng et al. [519]. The introduced supramolecular polymer melamine cyanurate behaves as a barrier to prevent graphene sheets from restacking, helping also as soft template to produce porous structure, while providing a N source for in-situ N-doping. The material showed specific capacitance of 335 F/g, which was 56.5% higher than pristine rGO (214 F/g). Capacitance retention reached 94.3% after 10000 cycles, demonstrating the good electrochemical stability of the electrodes. Poochai et al. [520] designed high performance coin-cell and pouch-cell SCs based on N-doped rGO electrodes with phenylenediamine-mediated organic electrolyte (1 M TEABF4 in CH3CN organic electrolyte). With optimum conditions, coin-cell SCs with N-rGO electrodes showed specific capacitance of 563 F/g at 1 A/g, as well as an energy of 149.4 Wh/kg for a power of 1382 W/kg. For the pouch cell SCs, specific capacitance was 340 F/g at 1 A/g, with energy of 77.2 Wh/kg for a power of 1278 W/kg. Liu et al. [521] investigated the synthesis of holey N-doped rGO modified with PANI for symmetric SCs. Holey N-doped rGO was synthesized via refluxing GO with NH3H2O and H2O2 as etching reagents, followed by chemical reduction (Fig. 26 a). The hybrid materials contained specific capacitance of 746 F/g at 1 A/g (Fig. 26b) and 97% capacitance retention after 2000 CV cycles. The holey N-doped rGO/PANI based symmetric SCs reveal specific capacitance of 510 F/g (1 A/g), specific capacitance retention of 74% after 2000 cycles (3 A/g) and energy density of 24.7 Wh/kg at a power density of 329.5 W/kg (Fig. 26c–e).

Asymmetric SCs designed using nanoarrays of PANI grown on GO (PANI-GO) as positive electrode and carbonized N-doped carbon nanoarrays grown on the surface of graphene (CPANI-graphene) as negative electrode have been demonstrated by Tabrizi et al. [522]. The symmetric cell based on PANI-GO displayed energy density of 40 Wh/kg, while the PANI-GO//CPANI-graphene asymmetric SCs exhibited energy density of 52 Wh/kg. Mangisetti et al. [523] investigated hierarchical N-doped porous carbon-graphene/PANI (porous carbon-graphene/PANI) hybrids synthesized by in-situ polymerization. The porous carbon was synthesized using biowaste bombax malabaricum seeds (carbon precursor) and the interconnected porous carbon-graphene was prepared by refluxing and activation process. The specific capacitance of porous carbon-graphene and porous carbon-graphene/PANI electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 were 610 and 1198 F/g (current density: 2 A/g), respectively. Symmetric SCs exhibited energy density of 61 Wh/kg and 117 Wh/kg in 1 M H2SO4 and 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolytes, respectively. Asymmetric SCs (positive electrode: porous carbon-graphene/PANi; negative electrode: porous N-doped carbon covered C3N4 nanosheets) contained an energy density of 97.5 Wh/kg in 0.5 M Na2SO4. The assembled symmetric and asymmetric SC devices displayed 94% and 91% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles, respectively. Gao et al. [524] reported hydrothermally synthesized N-doped rGO hydrogels in the presence of o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) with sulfuric acid for high performance SCs. The N-doped rGO hydrogels revealed specific capacitance of 519.8 F/g, energy density of 11.1 Wh/kg (power density of 14.1 kW/kg) and retained 93.3% of its initial capacitance after 15000 cycles.

Recently, inorganic compounds like the MXenes have grown much research interest in the field of energy storage and conversion. A series of graphene/MXene composites have been investigated as SC electrodes. As an example, Fan et al. [525] demonstrated the synthesis of MXene/holey graphene films, which displayed maximum volumetric capacitance of 1445 F/cm3. Moreover, the corresponding symmetric device also exhibited good volumetric energy density (of 38.6 Wh/L). In another work, freestanding, flexible MXene/rGO electrodes exhibited a volumetric capacitance of 1040 F/cm3 at the scan rate of 2 mV/s [526]. Like in the previous study, the corresponding symmetric device delivered a volumetric energy density of 32.6 Wh/L. Recently, Yun et al. [527] demonstrated the fabrication of wire-shaped SCs by integrating rGO with Ti3C2Tx MXene through the “bottom-up” layer-by-layer assembly process. Endowed with an enhanced charge storage mechanism, such SC assembly demonstrated a volumetric capacitance of 2193 F/cm3, which was found to be much higher than the one for activated carbon yarn. In another work, Ti3C2Tx MXene was combined with rGO to fabricate a stretchable SC, which displayed the volumetric capacitance of ∼490 F/cm3 [528], with the corresponding stretchable symmetric device exhibiting a volumetric capacitance of ∼90 F/cm3.

Ternary composites based on graphene derivatives and MXenes have also been explored. A sandwich-like Ti3C2Tx MXene/GO/Ni–Mn LDH composite exhibited a specific capacity of 241.9 mAh/g [529]. Most significantly, an asymmetric device with activated carbon as negative electrode displayed the enhanced energy density of 55.3 Wh/kg. In another work, 5 wt% of NiCoO2 nanosheets were integrated with MXene and rGO to develop an advanced SC electrode which exhibited a specific capacitance of 1614 F/g at a scan rate of 0.5 A/g [530]. An asymmetric device based on this ternary composite as positive and rGO/MXene as the negative electrode displayed the energy density of 45.15 Wh/kg as well as the capacity retention of 82.69% after 10000 charge/discharge cycles. In another work, Sikdar et al. synthesized a 3D hydrogel of Ti3C2Tx MXene and graphene through a Zn-induced gelation process. The composite exhibited a capacitance of 357 F/g at the scan rate of 10 mV/s and enhanced capacity retention (95.6% after 10000 cycles) [531]. An asymmetric device using this hydrogel as the negative and polyaniline/graphene hydrogel as positive electrode delivered an energy density of 30.3 Wh/kg. These studies clearly demonstrate that nanocomposites of MXene and graphene provide excellent electrochemical performance, being well suited for advanced SC electrodes.

Among the various conducting polymers, polythiophene, PANI, and PPy have been extensively combined with graphene materials for developing SC electrodes [532–534]. However, besides polymers, various copolymers have also been used. For example, a composite based on graphene and poly(aniline-co-pyrrole) [PANI-co-PPy] displayed a maximum capacitance of 351 F/g [535]. In another work, a 3D aerogel based on graphene and PANI-co-PPy exhibited a specific capacitance of 675 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g and a capacitance retention of 97.5% after 1000 cycles [536]. In this context, Liang et al. demonstrated an in-situ oxidative polymerization process for synthesizing rGO/PANI-co-PPy composites, which achieved the capacitance of 541 F/g and a capacitance retention of 86% after 500 cycles [537]. Recently, Liu et al. [538] reported a 3D core-shell structured rGO/PANI-co-PPy composite which displayed a maximum capacitance of 684.5 F/g as well as a capacitance retention of 88% after 1000 cycles. The enhanced electrochemical performance was attributed to the 3D structure of the electrode, which enhanced the stability of the co-polymer as well as generating a better synergy between rGO and the copolymer. In another study, thermally reduced rGO was combined with PANI-co-PPy to fabricate binder-free electrodes which exhibited a cycling stability of 103% after 10000 cycles [539]. Apart from this PANI-co-PPy, other copolymers like Poly(Pyrrole-co-Thiophene) [540], poly(aniline-co-m-aminophenol) [541] etc. have also demonstrated promising electrochemical performances in combination with rGO.

Over the past few years, SC technology has undergone a steady, step-by-step evolution. In their current development stage, SCs have already started to be seriously considered as suitable candidates for application as energy storage devices in real, day-to-day situations. Even though SCs have shown several tremendous advantages, like high power density, fast recharging, and long cycling life, other important characteristics of these devices still have to be improved for fully-fledged commercial application. Some of the major drawbacks which still need to be properly addressed in the near future are: (1) low compatibility between electrodes and electrolytes, (2) low energy density, (3) high cost (especially for electrolytes). In this review article, we have summarized the latest progress in the research and development of SC electrodes. The performances of EDLC-type and pseudocapacitive, as well as hybrid electrode materials have been comprehensively surveyed, highlighting emerging trends in SC technology.

Like any other energy storage devices, the performance of SCs is highly dependent on its electrode materials. High electrode surface areas are extremely desirable for improving the efficiency of SCs. In this context, 0D, 1D, and 2D carbon materials have been intensely developed. The rich redox activity taking place in the electrodes also leads to enhanced electrochemical performance. Taking this into consideration, metal oxides have been combined with carbon nanomaterials to form binary, ternary, as well as quaternary metal oxide electrodes. Over the past few years, following the relentless search for higher SC efficiency, various derivatives of conducting polymers and MXenes, have also been added to graphene. Furthermore, for the development of modern integrated energy storage-conversion systems, SCs have been successfully combined with other energy devices like secondary batteries, fuel cells and solar cells.

Overall, it can be concluded that the recent development in SC electrodes has been entirely satisfactory. However, for achieving advanced SCs capable to fulfill the ever increasing energy demands, a few hurdles still have to be overcome:
1.
One of the major problems of this century is environmental pollution. In this context, “greener” synthetic approaches for SC electrode materials are essential. In order to fulfil this requirement, biomass-derived carbon materials have been developed. However, these electrodes suffer from low conductivity, reduced surface area, and insufficient porosity. Therefore, optimizing the fabrication of these materials for SC electrodes is a big challenge for researchers.
2.
For conducting polymer-based electrodes, poor cycling stability is a significant problem, due to the considerable volume changes during charge/discharge cycling. In order to reduce the volume expansion, conducting polymers with different morphologies have been tried, such as hollow, tubular, fiber-like etc. However, these newly designed polymers suffer from low conductivity, low porosity, as well as low capacitive nature. The development of conducting polymers which are mechanically strong, without sacrificing their intrinsic capacitance is highly desirable.
3.
Self-discharging is another serious problem for commercial SCs. When compared to other energy storage devices, the self-discharging rate of SCs is quite high, and this should necessarily be reduced in order to improve their commercial prospects.
4.
The cost/efficiency ratio of SCs is quite high. Specifically, among the electrode candidates, some of the carbon materials, metal oxides like RuO2, and the MXenes are relatively expensive. Moreover, organic electrolytes are also costly. For successful device commercialization, the overall cost of SCs should be further reduced.
5.
Besides the electrodes, the separator and electrolytes also play vital roles in SC technology; however, most published research works are presently concentrated on the electrode materials. In general terms, aqueous devices suffer from low working potential, and the capacitance of solid-state devices is low. More research is highly desirable in the proper design of separators and selection of electrolytes for fabrication of high performance SCs.
6.
Controlling the mass loading of pseudocapacitive electrode materials is another delicate subject. Low mass loading obviously leads to poor capacitance. On the other hand, excessive mass loading of active materials may result in decreased electrolyte percolation and reduction of specific capacitance, which is inversely proportional to electrode mass. More studies directed to fine tune this parameter would be most welcome.
7.
In recent times, MOF-derived electrode materials exhibited satisfactory electrochemical performances. But the synthesis of these materials with controlled microstructures in a traditional, cost-effective way, is still challenging. Also, the practical application of such electrodes has not yet been extensively studied.
8.
Like in other energy storage/conversion devices, a big gap still exists between lab-developed fabrication processes and the commercially viable large scale fabrication methods. This discrepancy needs to be overcome for smooth commercialization of SCs.

In general, with recent developments in nanoscience and nanotechnology, the SC technology is also rapidly advancing. From activated carbon to graphene, from single metal oxides to ternary mixed metal oxides, from pure polymers to polymer derivatives; from 0D carbon to 2D MXenes; SC electrodes have been drastically improved in terms of electrochemical performance. We believe that, with the continuous improvement of existing materials, and with innovative research leading to new electrode compositions and architectures, the technology of SCs will be, in the near future, mature enough for the widespread adoption of SC devices in a huge variety of applications.
